name: The Confucian Analects
description: The collected sayings and teachings of Confucius (Kong Qiu, 551-479 BC), compiled by his disciples. Level 1 items contain the original Legge translation text of each individual teaching. Level 2 items are the 20 traditional books with thematic summaries.

attribution.note: English translation by James Legge (1893) from The Chinese Classics, Vol. 1. Text in the public domain.

items.name: The Master: Is it not pleasant to learn with a constant perseverance

sections.Teaching: The Master "Is it not pleasant to learn with a constant perseverance and application? "Is it not delightful to have friends coming from distant quarters? "Is he not a man of complete virtue, who feels no discomposure though men may take no note of him?"

items.name: The philosopher Yu: They are few who, being filial and fraternal, are...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Yu said, "They are few who, being filial and fraternal, are fond of offending against their superiors. There have been none, who, not liking to offend against their superiors, have been fond of stirring up confusion. "The superior man bends his attention to what is radical. That being established, all practical courses naturally grow up. Filial piety and fraternal submission,-are they not the root of all benevolent actions?"

items.name: The Master: Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom asso

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Fine words and an insinuating appearance are seldom associated with true virtue."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: I daily examine myself on three...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "I daily examine myself on three points:-whether, in transacting business for others, I may have been not faithful;-whether, in intercourse with friends, I may have been not sincere;-whether I may have not mastered and practiced the instructions of my teacher."

items.name: The Master: To rule a country of a thousand chariots, there must be

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "To rule a country of a thousand chariots, there must be reverent attention to business, and sincerity; economy in expenditure, and love for men; and the employment of the people at the proper seasons."

items.name: The Master: A youth, when at home, should be filial, and, abroad, re

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A youth, when at home, should be filial, and, abroad, respectful to his elders. He should be earnest and truthful. He should overflow in love to all, and cultivate the friendship of the good. When he has time and opportunity, after the performance of these things, he should employ them in polite studies."

items.name: Tsze-hsia: If a man withdraws his mind from the love of beauty, and

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia said, "If a man withdraws his mind from the love of beauty, and applies it as sincerely to the love of the virtuous; if, in serving his parents, he can exert his utmost strength; if, in serving his prince, he can devote his life; if, in his intercourse with his friends, his words are sincere:-although men say that he has not learned, I will certainly say that he has.

items.name: The Master: If the scholar be not grave, he will not call forth any

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If the scholar be not grave, he will not call forth any veneration, and his learning will not be solid. "Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. "Have no friends not equal to yourself. "When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: Let there be a careful attention to perform...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "Let there be a careful attention to perform the funeral rites to parents, and let them be followed when long gone with the ceremonies of sacrifice;-then the virtue of the people will resume its proper excellence."

items.name: Tsze-ch: in asked Tsze-kung saying, "When our master comes to any

sections.Teaching: Tsze-ch'in asked Tsze-kung saying, "When our master comes to any country, he does not fail to learn all about its government. Does he ask his information? or is it given to him?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: Our master is benign, upright, courteous, temperate, and

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Our master is benign, upright, courteous, temperate, and complaisant and thus he gets his information. The master's mode of asking information,-is it not different from that of other men?"

items.name: The Master: While a man's father is alive, look at the bent of his...

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "While a man's father is alive, look at the bent of his will; when his father is dead, look at his conduct. If for three years he does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial."

items.name: The philosopher Yu: In practicing the rules of propriety, a natural...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Yu said, "In practicing the rules of propriety, a natural ease is to be prized. In the ways prescribed by the ancient kings, this is the excellent quality, and in things small and great we follow them. "Yet it is not to be observed in all cases. If one, knowing how such ease should be prized, manifests it, without regulating it by the rules of propriety, this likewise is not to be done."

items.name: The philosopher Yu: When agreements are made according to what is...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Yu said, "When agreements are made according to what is right, what is spoken can be made good. When respect is shown according to what is proper, one keeps far from shame and disgrace. When the parties upon whom a man leans are proper persons to be intimate with, he can make them his guides and masters."

items.name: The Master: He who aims to be a man of complete virtue in his food d

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who aims to be a man of complete virtue in his food does not seek to gratify his appetite, nor in his dwelling place does he seek the appliances of ease; he is earnest in what he is doing, and careful in his speech; he frequents the company of men of principle that he may be rectified:-such a person may be said indeed to love to learn."

items.name: Tsze-kung: What do you pronounce concerning the poor man who yet do

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "What do you pronounce concerning the poor man who yet does not flatter, and the rich man who is not proud?" The Master replied, "They will do; but they are not equal to him, who, though poor, is yet cheerful, and to him, who, though rich, loves the rules of propriety."

items.name: Tsze-kung: replied, "It is said in the Book of Poetry, 'As you cut

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung replied, "It is said in the Book of Poetry, 'As you cut and then file, as you carve and then polish.'-The meaning is the same, I apprehend, as that which you have just expressed."

items.name: The Master: With one like Ts'ze, I can begin to talk about the odes

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "With one like Ts'ze, I can begin to talk about the odes. I told him one point, and he knew its proper sequence."

items.name: The Master: I will not be afflicted at men's not knowing me

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I will not be afflicted at men's not knowing me; I will be afflicted that I do not know men."

items.name: Book 1 — Xue Er (Learning)

sections.Brief: Foundational teachings on the love of learning, filial piety, and the character of the superior man. Confucius establishes that true virtue begins at home and radiates outward through sincerity and self-examination.

items.name: The Master: He who exercises government by means of his virtue may b

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who exercises government by means of his virtue may be compared to the north polar star, which keeps its place and all the stars turn towards it."

items.name: The Master: In the Book of Poetry are three hundred pieces, but the

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In the Book of Poetry are three hundred pieces, but the design of them all may be embraced in one sentence 'Having no depraved thoughts.'"

items.name: The Master: If the people be led by laws, and uniformity sought to b

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If the people be led by laws, and uniformity sought to be given them by punishments, they will try to avoid the punishment, but have no sense of shame. "If they be led by virtue, and uniformity sought to be given them by the rules of propriety, they will have the sense of shame, and moreover will become good."

items.name: The Master: At fifteen, I had my mind bent on learning

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "At fifteen, I had my mind bent on learning. "At thirty, I stood firm. "At forty, I had no doubts. "At fifty, I knew the decrees of Heaven. "At sixty, my ear was an obedient organ for the reception of truth. "At seventy, I could follow what my heart desired, without transgressing what was right."

items.name: Mang I: filial piety was

sections.Teaching: Mang I asked what filial piety was. The Master said, "It is not being disobedient."

items.name: Soon after, as Fan Ch'ih was driving him, the Master told

sections.Teaching: Soon after, as Fan Ch'ih was driving him, the Master told him, saying, "Mang-sun asked me what filial piety was, and I answered him,-'not being disobedient.'"

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: What did you mean

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih said, "What did you mean?" The Master replied, "That parents, when alive, be served according to propriety; that, when dead, they should be buried according to propriety; and that they should be sacrificed to according to propriety."

items.name: Mang Wu: filial piety was

sections.Teaching: Mang Wu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, "Parents are anxious lest their children should be sick."

items.name: Tsze-yu: filial piety was

sections.Teaching: Tsze-yu asked what filial piety was. The Master said, "The filial piety nowadays means the support of one's parents. But dogs and horses likewise are able to do something in the way of support;-without reverence, what is there to distinguish the one support given from the other?"

items.name: Tsze-hsia: filial piety was

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia asked what filial piety was. The Master said, "The difficulty is with the countenance. If, when their elders have any troublesome affairs, the young take the toil of them, and if, when the young have wine and food, they set them before their elders, is THIS to be considered filial piety?"

items.name: The Master: I have talked with Hui for a whole day, and he has not m

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I have talked with Hui for a whole day, and he has not made any objection to anything I said;-as if he were stupid. He has retired, and I have examined his conduct when away from me, and found him able to illustrate my teachings. Hui!-He is not stupid."

items.name: The Master: See what a man does

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "See what a man does. "Mark his motives. "Examine in what things he rests. "How can a man conceal his character? How can a man conceal his character?"

items.name: The Master: If a man keeps cherishing his old knowledge, so as conti

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a man keeps cherishing his old knowledge, so as continually to be acquiring new, he may be a teacher of others."

items.name: The Master: The accomplished scholar is not a utensil

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The accomplished scholar is not a utensil."

items.name: Tsze-kung: constituted the superior man

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked what constituted the superior man. The Master said, "He acts before he speaks, and afterwards speaks according to his actions."

items.name: The Master: The superior man is catholic and not partisan

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man is catholic and not partisan. The mean man is partisan and not catholic."

items.name: The Master: Learning without thought is labor lost

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Learning without thought is labor lost; thought without learning is perilous."

items.name: The Master: The study of strange doctrines is injurious indeed

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The study of strange doctrines is injurious indeed!"

items.name: The Master: Yu, shall I teach you what knowledge is

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Yu, shall I teach you what knowledge is? When you know a thing, to hold that you know it; and when you do not know a thing, to allow that you do not know it;-this is knowledge."

items.name: Tsze-chang: was learning with a view to official emolument

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang was learning with a view to official emolument.

items.name: The Master: Hear much and put aside the points of which you stand in

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Hear much and put aside the points of which you stand in doubt, while you speak cautiously at the same time of the others:-then you will afford few occasions for blame. See much and put aside the things which seem perilous, while you are cautious at the same time in carrying the others into practice: then you will have few occasions for repentance. When one gives few occasions for blame in his words, and few occasions for repentance in his conduct, he is in the way to get emolument."

items.name: The Duke Ai asked, saying, "What should be done in order

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ai asked, saying, "What should be done in order to secure the submission of the people?" Confucius replied, "Advance the upright and set aside the crooked, then the people will submit. Advance the crooked and set aside the upright, then the people will not submit."

items.name: Chi K: ang asked how to cause the people to reverence their rule

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang asked how to cause the people to reverence their ruler, to be faithful to him, and to go on to nerve themselves to virtue. The Master said, "Let him preside over them with gravity;-then they will reverence him. Let him be final and kind to all;-then they will be faithful to him. Let him advance the good and teach the incompetent;-then they will eagerly seek to be virtuous."

items.name: Some one addressed Confucius, saying, "Sir, why are you n

sections.Teaching: Some one addressed Confucius, saying, "Sir, why are you not engaged in the government?"

items.name: The Master: What does the Shu-ching say of filial piety

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "What does the Shu-ching say of filial piety?-'You are final, you discharge your brotherly duties. These qualities are displayed in government.' This then also constitutes the exercise of government. Why must there be THAT-making one be in the government?"

items.name: The Master: I do not know how a man without truthfulness is to get on

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I do not know how a man without truthfulness is to get on. How can a large carriage be made to go without the crossbar for yoking the oxen to, or a small carriage without the arrangement for yoking the horses?"

items.name: Tsze-chang: the affairs of ten ages after could be known

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked whether the affairs of ten ages after could be known.

items.name: Confucius: The Yin dynasty followed the regulations of the Hsia: wh

sections.Teaching: Confucius said, "The Yin dynasty followed the regulations of the Hsia: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. The Chau dynasty has followed the regulations of Yin: wherein it took from or added to them may be known. Some other may follow the Chau, but though it should be at the distance of a hundred ages, its affairs may be known."

items.name: The Master: For a man to sacrifice to a spirit which does not belong

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "For a man to sacrifice to a spirit which does not belong to him is flattery. "To see what is right and not to do it is want of courage."

items.name: Book 2 — Wei Zheng (Governing)

sections.Brief: Teachings on governance through moral example rather than force. The Master compares the virtuous ruler to the North Star — fixed in place while all else revolves around it. Includes reflections on the Book of Poetry and the stages of Confucius's own life.

items.name: Confucius: of the head of the Chi family, who had eight rows of pan

sections.Teaching: Confucius said of the head of the Chi family, who had eight rows of pantomimes in his area, "If he can bear to do this, what may he not bear to do?"

items.name: The three families used the Yungode, while the vessels we

sections.Teaching: The three families used the Yungode, while the vessels were being removed, at the conclusion of the sacrifice. The Master said, "'Assisting are the princes;-the son of heaven looks profound and grave';-what application can these words have in the hall of the three families?"

items.name: The Master: If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with the rites of propriety? If a man be without the virtues proper to humanity, what has he to do with music?"

items.name: Lin Fang: was the first thing to be attended to in ceremonies

sections.Teaching: Lin Fang asked what was the first thing to be attended to in ceremonies.

items.name: The Master: A great question indeed

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A great question indeed! "In festive ceremonies, it is better to be sparing than extravagant. In the ceremonies of mourning, it is better that there be deep sorrow than in minute attention to observances."

items.name: The Master: The rude tribes of the east and north have their princes

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The rude tribes of the east and north have their princes, and are not like the States of our great land which are without them."

items.name: The chief of the Chi family was about to sacrifice to the

sections.Teaching: The chief of the Chi family was about to sacrifice to the T'ai mountain. The Master said to Zan Yu, "Can you not save him from this?" He answered, "I cannot." Confucius said, "Alas! will you say that the T'ai mountain is not so discerning as Lin Fang?"

items.name: The Master: The student of virtue has no contentions

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The student of virtue has no contentions. If it be said he cannot avoid them, shall this be in archery? But he bows complaisantly to his competitors; thus he ascends the hall, descends, and exacts the forfeit of drinking. In his contention, he is still the Chun-tsze."

items.name: Tsze-hsia: asked, saying, "What is the meaning of the passage-'The

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia asked, saying, "What is the meaning of the passage-'The pretty dimples of her artful smile! The well-defined black and white of her eye! The plain ground for the colors?'"

items.name: The Master: The business of laying on the colors follows the prepara

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The business of laying on the colors follows the preparation of the plain ground." "Ceremonies then are a subsequent thing?" The Master said, "It is Shang who can bring out my meaning. Now I can begin to talk about the odes with him."

items.name: The Master: I could describe the ceremonies of the Hsia dynasty, but

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I could describe the ceremonies of the Hsia dynasty, but Chi cannot sufficiently attest my words. I could describe the ceremonies of the Yin dynasty, but Sung cannot sufficiently attest my words. They cannot do so because of the insufficiency of their records and wise men. If those were sufficient, I could adduce them in support of my words."

items.name: The Master: At the great sacrifice, after the pouring out of the lib

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "At the great sacrifice, after the pouring out of the libation, I have no wish to look on."

items.name: Some one asked the meaning of the great sacrifice

sections.Teaching: Some one asked the meaning of the great sacrifice. The Master said, "I do not know. He who knew its meaning would find it as easy to govern the kingdom as to look on this"-pointing to his palm.

items.name: He sacrificed to the dead, as if they were present

sections.Teaching: He sacrificed to the dead, as if they were present. He sacrificed to the spirits, as if the spirits were present.

items.name: The Master: I consider my not being present at the sacrifice, as if

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I consider my not being present at the sacrifice, as if I did not sacrifice."

items.name: Wang-sun Chia: asked, saying, "What is the meaning of the saying, 'It...

sections.Teaching: Wang-sun Chia asked, saying, "What is the meaning of the saying, 'It is better to pay court to the furnace then to the southwest corner?'"

items.name: The Master: Not so

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Not so. He who offends against Heaven has none to whom he can pray."

items.name: The Master: Chau had the advantage of viewing the two past dynasties

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Chau had the advantage of viewing the two past dynasties. How complete and elegant are its regulations! I follow Chau."

items.name: The Master: , when he entered the grand temple, asked about everything

sections.Teaching: The Master, when he entered the grand temple, asked about everything. Some one said, "Who say that the son of the man of Tsau knows the rules of propriety! He has entered the grand temple and asks about everything." The Master heard the remark, and said, "This is a rule of propriety."

items.name: The Master: In archery it is not going through the leather which is

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In archery it is not going through the leather which is the principal thing;-because people's strength is not equal. This was the old way."

items.name: Tsze-kung: wished to do away with the offering of a sheep connected

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung wished to do away with the offering of a sheep connected with the inauguration of the first day of each month.

items.name: The Master: Ts'ze, you love the sheep

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Ts'ze, you love the sheep; I love the ceremony."

items.name: The Master: The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving one's prince is accounted by people to be flattery."

items.name: The Duke Ting asked how a prince should employ his minist

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ting asked how a prince should employ his ministers, and how ministers should serve their prince. Confucius replied, "A prince should employ his minister according to according to the rules of propriety; ministers should serve their prince with faithfulness."

items.name: The Master: The Kwan Tsu is expressive of enjoyment without being li

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The Kwan Tsu is expressive of enjoyment without being licentious, and of grief without being hurtfully excessive."

items.name: The Duke Ai asked Tsai Wo about the altars of the spirits

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ai asked Tsai Wo about the altars of the spirits of the land. Tsai Wo replied, "The Hsia sovereign planted the pine tree about them; the men of the Yin planted the cypress; and the men of the Chau planted the chestnut tree, meaning thereby to cause the people to be in awe."

items.name: When the Master heard it, he said, "Things that are done,

sections.Teaching: When the Master heard it, he said, "Things that are done, it is needless to speak about; things that have had their course, it is needless to remonstrate about; things that are past, it is needless to blame."

items.name: The Master: Small indeed was the capacity of Kwan Chung

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Small indeed was the capacity of Kwan Chung!"

items.name: Some one said, "Was Kwan Chung parsimonious

sections.Teaching: Some one said, "Was Kwan Chung parsimonious?" "Kwan," was the reply, "had the San Kwei, and his officers performed no double duties; how can he be considered parsimonious?" "Then, did Kwan Chung know the rules of propriety?" The Master said, "The princes of States have a screen intercepting the view at their gates. Kwan had likewise a screen at his gate. The princes of States on any friendly meeting between two of them, had a stand on which to place their inverted cups. Kwan had also such a stand. If Kwan knew the rules of propriety, who does not know them?"

items.name: The Master: instructing the grand music master of Lu said, "How to p

sections.Teaching: The Master instructing the grand music master of Lu said, "How to play music may be known. At the commencement of the piece, all the parts should sound together. As it proceeds, they should be in harmony while severally distinct and flowing without break, and thus on to the conclusion."

items.name: The border warden at Yi requested to be introduced to the

sections.Teaching: The border warden at Yi requested to be introduced to the Master, saying, "When men of superior virtue have come to this, I have never been denied the privilege of seeing them." The followers of the sage introduced him, and when he came out from the interview, he said, "My friends, why are you distressed by your master's loss of office? The kingdom has long been without the principles of truth and right; Heaven is going to use your master as a bell with its wooden tongue."

items.name: The Master: of the Shao that it was perfectly beautiful and also per

sections.Teaching: The Master said of the Shao that it was perfectly beautiful and also perfectly good. He said of the Wu that it was perfectly beautiful but not perfectly good.

items.name: The Master: High station filled without indulgent generosity

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "High station filled without indulgent generosity; ceremonies performed without reverence; mourning conducted without sorrow;-wherewith should I contemplate such ways?"

items.name: Book 3 — Ba Yi (Eight Rows of Dance)

sections.Brief: Proper observance of ritual and ceremony. Confucius criticizes those who perform the forms of ritual without sincerity, and those who overreach their station in ceremonies reserved for higher ranks.

items.name: The Master: It is virtuous manners which constitute the excellence o

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is virtuous manners which constitute the excellence of a neighborhood. If a man in selecting a residence do not fix on one where such prevail, how can he be wise?"

items.name: The Master: Those who are without virtue cannot abide long either in

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Those who are without virtue cannot abide long either in a condition of poverty and hardship, or in a condition of enjoyment. The virtuous rest in virtue; the wise desire virtue."

items.name: The Master: It is only the truly virtuous man, who can love, or who

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is only the truly virtuous man, who can love, or who can hate, others."

items.name: The Master: If the will be set on virtue, there will be no practice

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If the will be set on virtue, there will be no practice of wickedness."

items.name: The Master: Riches and honors are what men desire

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Riches and honors are what men desire. If they cannot be obtained in the proper way, they should not be held. Poverty and meanness are what men dislike. If they cannot be avoided in the proper way, they should not be avoided. "If a superior man abandon virtue, how can he fulfill the requirements of that name? "The superior man does not, even for the space of a single meal, act contrary to virtue. In moments of haste, he cleaves to it. In seasons of danger, he cleaves to it."

items.name: The Master: I have not seen a person who loved virtue, or one who ha

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I have not seen a person who loved virtue, or one who hated what was not virtuous. He who loved virtue, would esteem nothing above it. He who hated what is not virtuous, would practice virtue in such a way that he would not allow anything that is not virtuous to approach his person. "Is any one able for one day to apply his strength to virtue? I have not seen the case in which his strength would be insufficient. "Should there possibly be any such case, I have not seen it."

items.name: The Master: The faults of men are characteristic of the class to whi

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The faults of men are characteristic of the class to which they belong. By observing a man's faults, it may be known that he is virtuous."

items.name: The Master: If a man in the morning hear the right way, he may die i

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a man in the morning hear the right way, he may die in the evening hear regret."

items.name: The Master: A scholar, whose mind is set on truth, and who is ashame

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A scholar, whose mind is set on truth, and who is ashamed of bad clothes and bad food, is not fit to be discoursed with."

items.name: The Master: The superior man, in the world, does not set his mind ei

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man, in the world, does not set his mind either for anything, or against anything; what is right he will follow."

items.name: The Master: The superior man thinks of virtue

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man thinks of virtue; the small man thinks of comfort. The superior man thinks of the sanctions of law; the small man thinks of favors which he may receive."

items.name: The Master: : "He who acts with a constant view to his own advantage

sections.Teaching: The Master said: "He who acts with a constant view to his own advantage will be much murmured against."

items.name: The Master: If a prince is able to govern his kingdom with the compl

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a prince is able to govern his kingdom with the complaisance proper to the rules of propriety, what difficulty will he have? If he cannot govern it with that complaisance, what has he to do with the rules of propriety?"

items.name: The Master: A man should say, I am not concerned that I have no plac

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A man should say, I am not concerned that I have no place, I am concerned how I may fit myself for one. I am not concerned that I am not known, I seek to be worthy to be known."

items.name: The Master: Shan, my doctrine is that of an all-pervading unity

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Shan, my doctrine is that of an all-pervading unity." The disciple Tsang replied, "Yes."

items.name: The Master: went out, and the other disciples asked, saying, "What do

sections.Teaching: The Master went out, and the other disciples asked, saying, "What do his words mean?" Tsang said, "The doctrine of our master is to be true to the principles-of our nature and the benevolent exercise of them to others,-this and nothing more."

items.name: The Master: The mind of the superior man is conversant with righteou

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The mind of the superior man is conversant with righteousness; the mind of the mean man is conversant with gain."

items.name: The Master: When we see men of worth, we should think of equaling them

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When we see men of worth, we should think of equaling them; when we see men of a contrary character, we should turn inwards and examine ourselves."

items.name: The Master: In serving his parents, a son may remonstrate with them,

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In serving his parents, a son may remonstrate with them, but gently; when he sees that they do not incline to follow his advice, he shows an increased degree of reverence, but does not abandon his purpose; and should they punish him, he does not allow himself to murmur."

items.name: The Master: While his parents are alive, the son may not go abroad t

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "While his parents are alive, the son may not go abroad to a distance. If he does go abroad, he must have a fixed place to which he goes."

items.name: The Master: If the son for three years does not alter from the way o

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If the son for three years does not alter from the way of his father, he may be called filial."

items.name: The Master: The years of parents may by no means not be kept in the

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The years of parents may by no means not be kept in the memory, as an occasion at once for joy and for fear."

items.name: The Master: The reason why the ancients did not readily give utteran

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The reason why the ancients did not readily give utterance to their words, was that they feared lest their actions should not come up to them."

items.name: The Master: The cautious seldom err

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The cautious seldom err."

items.name: The Master: The superior man wishes to be slow in his speech and ear

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man wishes to be slow in his speech and earnest in his conduct."

items.name: The Master: Virtue is not left to stand alone

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Virtue is not left to stand alone. He who practices it will have neighbors."

items.name: Tsze-yu: In serving a prince, frequent remonstrances lead to disg

sections.Teaching: Tsze-yu said, "In serving a prince, frequent remonstrances lead to disgrace. Between friends, frequent reproofs make the friendship distant."

items.name: Book 4 — Li Ren (Living with Virtue)

sections.Brief: The virtue of ren (benevolence/humaneness) as the central aim of life. The Master teaches that the truly virtuous find their home in ren, while others merely visit it when convenient.

items.name: The Master: of Kung-ye Ch'ang that he might be wived

sections.Teaching: The Master said of Kung-ye Ch'ang that he might be wived; although he was put in bonds, he had not been guilty of any crime. Accordingly, he gave him his own daughter to wife.

items.name: Of Nan Yung he said that if the country were well governe

sections.Teaching: Of Nan Yung he said that if the country were well governed he would not be out of office, and if it were in governed, he would escape punishment and disgrace. He gave him the daughter of his own elder brother to wife.

items.name: The Master: of Tsze-chien, "Of superior virtue indeed is such a man

sections.Teaching: The Master said of Tsze-chien, "Of superior virtue indeed is such a man! If there were not virtuous men in Lu, how could this man have acquired this character?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: asked, "What do you say of me, Ts'ze

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked, "What do you say of me, Ts'ze!" The Master said, "You are a utensil." "What utensil?" "A gemmed sacrificial utensil."

items.name: Some one said, "Yung is truly virtuous, but he is not rea

sections.Teaching: Some one said, "Yung is truly virtuous, but he is not ready with his tongue."

items.name: The Master: What is the good of being ready with the tongue

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "What is the good of being ready with the tongue? They who encounter men with smartness of speech for the most part procure themselves hatred. I know not whether he be truly virtuous, but why should he show readiness of the tongue?"

items.name: The Master: was wishing Ch'i-tiao K'ai to enter an official employment

sections.Teaching: The Master was wishing Ch'i-tiao K'ai to enter an official employment. He replied, "I am not yet able to rest in the assurance of this." The Master was pleased.

items.name: The Master: My doctrines make no way

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "My doctrines make no way. I will get upon a raft, and float about on the sea. He that will accompany me will be Yu, I dare say." Tsze-lu hearing this was glad, upon which the Master said, "Yu is fonder of daring than I am. He does not exercise his judgment upon matters."

items.name: Mang Wu: Tsze-lu, whether he was perfectly virtuous

sections.Teaching: Mang Wu asked about Tsze-lu, whether he was perfectly virtuous. The Master said, "I do not know."

items.name: He asked again, when the Master replied, "In a kingdom of

sections.Teaching: He asked again, when the Master replied, "In a kingdom of a thousand chariots, Yu might be employed to manage the military levies, but I do not know whether he be perfectly virtuous." "And what do you say of Ch'iu?" The Master replied, "In a city of a thousand families, or a clan of a hundred chariots, Ch'iu might be employed as governor, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous." "What do you say of Ch'ih?" The Master replied, "With his sash girt and standing in a court, Ch'ih might be employed to converse with the visitors and guests, but I do not know whether he is perfectly virtuous."

items.name: The Master: to Tsze-kung, "Which do you consider superior, yourself

sections.Teaching: The Master said to Tsze-kung, "Which do you consider superior, yourself or Hui?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: replied, "How dare I compare myself with Hui

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung replied, "How dare I compare myself with Hui? Hui hears one point and knows all about a subject; I hear one point, and know a second."

items.name: The Master: You are not equal to him

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "You are not equal to him. I grant you, you are not equal to him."

items.name: Tsai Yu: being asleep during the daytime, the Master said, "Rotten

sections.Teaching: Tsai Yu being asleep during the daytime, the Master said, "Rotten wood cannot be carved; a wall of dirty earth will not receive the trowel. This Yu,-what is the use of my reproving him?"

items.name: The Master: At first, my way with men was to hear their words, and g

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "At first, my way with men was to hear their words, and give them credit for their conduct. Now my way is to hear their words, and look at their conduct. It is from Yu that I have learned to make this change."

items.name: The Master: I have not seen a firm and unbending man

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I have not seen a firm and unbending man." Some one replied, "There is Shan Ch'ang." "Ch'ang," said the Master, "is under the influence of his passions; how can he be pronounced firm and unbending?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: What I do not wish men to do to me, I also wish not to d

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "What I do not wish men to do to me, I also wish not to do to men." The Master said, "Ts'ze, you have not attained to that."

items.name: Tsze-kung: The Master's personal displays of his principles and ord

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "The Master's personal displays of his principles and ordinary descriptions of them may be heard. His discourses about man's nature, and the way of Heaven, cannot be heard."

items.name: When Tsze-lu heard anything, if he had not yet succeeded

sections.Teaching: When Tsze-lu heard anything, if he had not yet succeeded in carrying it into practice, he was only afraid lest he should hear something else.

items.name: Tsze-kung: asked, saying, "On what ground did Kung-wan get that titl

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked, saying, "On what ground did Kung-wan get that title of Wan?"

items.name: The Master: He was of an active nature and yet fond of learning, and

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He was of an active nature and yet fond of learning, and he was not ashamed to ask and learn of his inferiors!-On these grounds he has been styled Wan."

items.name: The Master: of Tsze-ch'an that he had four of the characteristics of

sections.Teaching: The Master said of Tsze-ch'an that he had four of the characteristics of a superior man-in his conduct of himself, he was humble; in serving his superior, he was respectful; in nourishing the people, he was kind; in ordering the people, he was just."

items.name: The Master: Yen P'ing knew well how to maintain friendly intercourse

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Yen P'ing knew well how to maintain friendly intercourse. The acquaintance might be long, but he showed the same respect as at first."

items.name: The Master: Tsang Wan kept a large tortoise in a house, on the capit

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Tsang Wan kept a large tortoise in a house, on the capitals of the pillars of which he had hills made, and with representations of duckweed on the small pillars above the beams supporting the rafters.-Of what sort was his wisdom?"

items.name: Tsze-chang: asked, saying, "The minister Tsze-wan thrice took office,

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked, saying, "The minister Tsze-wan thrice took office, and manifested no joy in his countenance. Thrice he retired from office, and manifested no displeasure. He made it a point to inform the new minister of the way in which he had conducted the government; what do you say of him?" The Master replied. "He was loyal." "Was he perfectly virtuous?" "I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?"

items.name: Tsze-chang: proceeded, "When the officer Ch'ui killed the prince of

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang proceeded, "When the officer Ch'ui killed the prince of Ch'i, Ch'an Wan, though he was the owner of forty horses, abandoned them and left the country. Coming to another state, he said, 'They are here like our great officer, Ch'ui,' and left it. He came to a second state, and with the same observation left it also;-what do you say of him?" The Master replied, "He was pure." "Was he perfectly virtuous?" "I do not know. How can he be pronounced perfectly virtuous?"

items.name: Chi Wan: thought thrice, and then acted

sections.Teaching: Chi Wan thought thrice, and then acted. When the Master was informed of it, he said, "Twice may do."

items.name: The Master: When good order prevailed in his country, Ning Wu acted

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When good order prevailed in his country, Ning Wu acted the part of a wise man. When his country was in disorder, he acted the part of a stupid man. Others may equal his wisdom, but they cannot equal his stupidity."

items.name: When the Master was in Ch'an, he said, "Let me return

sections.Teaching: When the Master was in Ch'an, he said, "Let me return! Let me return! The little children of my school are ambitious and too hasty. They are accomplished and complete so far, but they do not know how to restrict and shape themselves."

items.name: The Master: Po-i and Shu-ch'i did not keep the former wickednesses o

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Po-i and Shu-ch'i did not keep the former wickednesses of men in mind, and hence the resentments directed towards them were few."

items.name: The Master: Who says of Weishang Kao that he is upright

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Who says of Weishang Kao that he is upright? One begged some vinegar of him, and he begged it of a neighbor and gave it to the man."

items.name: The Master: Fine words, an insinuating appearance, and excessive res

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Fine words, an insinuating appearance, and excessive respect;-Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of them. I also am ashamed of them. To conceal resentment against a person, and appear friendly with him;-Tso Ch'iu-ming was ashamed of such conduct. I also am ashamed of it."

items.name: Yen Yuan: and Chi Lu being by his side, the Master said to them, "

sections.Teaching: Yen Yuan and Chi Lu being by his side, the Master said to them, "Come, let each of you tell his wishes."

items.name: Tsze-lu: I should like, having chariots and horses, and light fur

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu said, "I should like, having chariots and horses, and light fur clothes, to share them with my friends, and though they should spoil them, I would not be displeased."

items.name: Yen Yuan: I should like not to boast of my excellence, nor to make

sections.Teaching: Yen Yuan said, "I should like not to boast of my excellence, nor to make a display of my meritorious deeds."

items.name: Tsze-lu: then said, "I should like, sir, to hear your wishes

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu then said, "I should like, sir, to hear your wishes." The Master said, "They are, in regard to the aged, to give them rest; in regard to friends, to show them sincerity; in regard to the young, to treat them tenderly."

items.name: The Master: It is all over

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is all over. I have not yet seen one who could perceive his faults, and inwardly accuse himself."

items.name: The Master: In a hamlet of ten families, there may be found one hono

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In a hamlet of ten families, there may be found one honorable and sincere as I am, but not so fond of learning."

items.name: Book 5 — Gong Ye Chang

sections.Brief: Character assessments of disciples and contemporaries. Confucius evaluates the strengths and shortcomings of various followers, illustrating virtue and its absence through concrete examples.

items.name: The Master: There is Yung

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There is Yung!-He might occupy the place of a prince."

items.name: Chung-kung: Tsze-sang Po-tsze

sections.Teaching: Chung-kung asked about Tsze-sang Po-tsze. The Master said, "He may pass. He does not mind small matters."

items.name: Chung-kung: If a man cherish in himself a reverential feeling of the

sections.Teaching: Chung-kung said, "If a man cherish in himself a reverential feeling of the necessity of attention to business, though he may be easy in small matters in his government of the people, that may be allowed. But if he cherish in himself that easy feeling, and also carry it out in his practice, is not such an easymode of procedure excessive?"

items.name: The Master: Yung's words are right

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Yung's words are right."

items.name: The Duke Ai asked which of the disciples loved to learn

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ai asked which of the disciples loved to learn.

items.name: Confucius: replied to him, "There was Yen Hui

sections.Teaching: Confucius replied to him, "There was Yen Hui; he loved to learn. He did not transfer his anger; he did not repeat a fault. Unfortunately, his appointed time was short and he died; and now there is not such another. I have not yet heard of any one who loves to learn as he did."

items.name: Tsze-hwa: being employed on a mission to Ch'i, the disciple Zan re

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hwa being employed on a mission to Ch'i, the disciple Zan requested grain for his mother. The Master said, "Give her a fu." Yen requested more. "Give her a yi," said the Master. Yen gave her five ping.

items.name: The Master: When Ch'ih was proceeding to Ch'i, he had fat horses to

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When Ch'ih was proceeding to Ch'i, he had fat horses to his carriage, and wore light furs. I have heard that a superior man helps the distressed, but does not add to the wealth of the rich."

items.name: Yu: an Sze being made governor of his town by the Master, he

sections.Teaching: Yuan Sze being made governor of his town by the Master, he gave him nine hundred measures of grain, but Sze declined them.

items.name: The Master: Do not decline them

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Do not decline them. May you not give them away in the neighborhoods, hamlets, towns, and villages?"

items.name: The Master: , speaking of Chung-kung, said, "If the calf of a brindle

sections.Teaching: The Master, speaking of Chung-kung, said, "If the calf of a brindled cow be red and homed, although men may not wish to use it, would the spirits of the mountains and rivers put it aside?"

items.name: The Master: Such was Hui that for three months there would be nothin

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Such was Hui that for three months there would be nothing in his mind contrary to perfect virtue. The others may attain to this on some days or in some months, but nothing more."

items.name: Chi K: ang asked about Chung-yu, whether he was fit to be employ

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang asked about Chung-yu, whether he was fit to be employed as an officer of government. The Master said, "Yu is a man of decision; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?" K'ang asked, "Is Ts'ze fit to be employed as an officer of government?" and was answered, "Ts'ze is a man of intelligence; what difficulty would he find in being an officer of government?" And to the same question about Ch'iu the Master gave the same reply, saying, "Ch'iu is a man of various ability."

items.name: The chief of the Chi family sent to ask Min Tsze-ch'ien t

sections.Teaching: The chief of the Chi family sent to ask Min Tsze-ch'ien to be governor of Pi. Min Tszech'ien said, "Decline the offer for me politely. If any one come again to me with a second invitation, I shall be obliged to go and live on the banks of the Wan."

items.name: Po-niu being ill, the Master went to ask for him

sections.Teaching: Po-niu being ill, the Master went to ask for him. He took hold of his hand through the window, and said, "It is killing him. It is the appointment of Heaven, alas! That such a man should have such a sickness! That such a man should have such a sickness!"

items.name: The Master: Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui! With a single bamboo dish of rice, a single gourd dish of drink, and living in his mean narrow lane, while others could not have endured the distress, he did not allow his joy to be affected by it. Admirable indeed was the virtue of Hui!"

items.name: Yen Ch: iu said, "It is not that I do not delight in your doctri

sections.Teaching: Yen Ch'iu said, "It is not that I do not delight in your doctrines, but my strength is insufficient." The Master said, "Those whose strength is insufficient give over in the middle of the way but now you limit yourself."

items.name: The Master: to Tsze-hsia, "Do you be a scholar after the style of th

sections.Teaching: The Master said to Tsze-hsia, "Do you be a scholar after the style of the superior man, and not after that of the mean man."

items.name: Tsze-yu: being governor of Wu-ch'ang, the Master said to him, "Hav

sections.Teaching: Tsze-yu being governor of Wu-ch'ang, the Master said to him, "Have you got good men there?" He answered, "There is Tan-t'ai Miehming, who never in walking takes a short cut, and never comes to my office, excepting on public business."

items.name: The Master: Mang Chih-fan does not boast of his merit

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Mang Chih-fan does not boast of his merit. Being in the rear on an occasion of flight, when they were about to enter the gate, he whipped up his horse, saying, "It is not that I dare to be last. My horse would not advance."

items.name: The Master: Without the specious speech of the litanist T'o and the

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Without the specious speech of the litanist T'o and the beauty of the prince Chao of Sung, it is difficult to escape in the present age."

items.name: The Master: Who can go out but by the door

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Who can go out but by the door? How is it that men will not walk according to these ways?"

items.name: The Master: Where the solid qualities are in excess of accomplishmen

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Where the solid qualities are in excess of accomplishments, we have rusticity; where the accomplishments are in excess of the solid qualities, we have the manners of a clerk. When the accomplishments and solid qualities are equally blended, we then have the man of virtue."

items.name: The Master: Man is born for uprightness

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Man is born for uprightness. If a man lose his uprightness, and yet live, his escape from death is the effect of mere good fortune."

items.name: The Master: They who know the truth are not equal to those who love

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "They who know the truth are not equal to those who love it, and they who love it are not equal to those who delight in it."

items.name: The Master: To those whose talents are above mediocrity, the highest

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "To those whose talents are above mediocrity, the highest subjects may be announced. To those who are below mediocrity, the highest subjects may not be announced."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: constituted wisdom

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih asked what constituted wisdom. The Master said, "To give one's self earnestly to the duties due to men, and, while respecting spiritual beings, to keep aloof from them, may be called wisdom." He asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, "The man of virtue makes the difficulty to be overcome his first business, and success only a subsequent consideration;-this may be called perfect virtue."

items.name: The Master: The wise find pleasure in water

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The wise find pleasure in water; the virtuous find pleasure in hills. The wise are active; the virtuous are tranquil. The wise are joyful; the virtuous are long-lived."

items.name: The Master: Ch'i, by one change, would come to the State of Lu

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Ch'i, by one change, would come to the State of Lu. Lu, by one change, would come to a State where true principles predominated."

items.name: The Master: A cornered vessel without corners-a strange cornered...

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A cornered vessel without corners-a strange cornered vessel! A strange cornered vessel!"

items.name: Tsai Wo: asked, saying, "A benevolent man, though it be told him,

sections.Teaching: Tsai Wo asked, saying, "A benevolent man, though it be told him,-'There is a man in the well" will go in after him, I suppose." Confucius said, "Why should he do so?" A superior man may be made to go to the well, but he cannot be made to go down into it. He may be imposed upon, but he cannot be fooled."

items.name: The Master: The superior man, extensively studying all learning, and

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man, extensively studying all learning, and keeping himself under the restraint of the rules of propriety, may thus likewise not overstep what is right."

items.name: The Master: having visited Nan-tsze, Tsze-lu was displeased, on which

sections.Teaching: The Master having visited Nan-tsze, Tsze-lu was displeased, on which the Master swore, saying, "Wherein I have done improperly, may Heaven reject me, may Heaven reject me!"

items.name: The Master: Perfect is the virtue which is according to the Constant

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Perfect is the virtue which is according to the Constant Mean! Rare for a long time has been its practice among the people."

items.name: Tsze-kung: Suppose the case of a man extensively conferring benefit

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Suppose the case of a man extensively conferring benefits on the people, and able to assist all, what would you say of him? Might he be called perfectly virtuous?" The Master said, "Why speak only of virtue in connection with him? Must he not have the qualities of a sage? Even Yao and Shun were still solicitous about this. "Now the man of perfect virtue, wishing to be established himself, seeks also to establish others; wishing to be enlarged himself, he seeks also to enlarge others. "To be able to judge of others by what is nigh in ourselves;-this may be called the art of virtue."

items.name: Book 6 — Yong Ye

sections.Brief: Further character studies and discussions of the balanced man. The Master praises simplicity over cleverness and argues that substance should predominate over ornament — but that both are needed.

items.name: The Master: A transmitter and not a maker, believing in and loving t

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A transmitter and not a maker, believing in and loving the ancients, I venture to compare myself with our old P'ang."

items.name: The Master: The silent treasuring up of knowledge

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The silent treasuring up of knowledge; learning without satiety; and instructing others without being wearied:-which one of these things belongs to me?"

items.name: The Master: The leaving virtue without proper cultivation

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The leaving virtue without proper cultivation; the not thoroughly discussing what is learned; not being able to move towards righteousness of which a knowledge is gained; and not being able to change what is not good:-these are the things which occasion me solicitude."

items.name: When the Master was unoccupied with business, his manner

sections.Teaching: When the Master was unoccupied with business, his manner was easy, and he looked pleased.

items.name: The Master: Extreme is my decay

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Extreme is my decay. For a long time, I have not dreamed, as I was wont to do, that I saw the duke of Chau."

items.name: The Master: Let the will be set on the path of duty

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Let the will be set on the path of duty. "Let every attainment in what is good be firmly grasped. "Let perfect virtue be accorded with. "Let relaxation and enjoyment be found in the polite arts."

items.name: The Master: From the man bringing his bundle of dried flesh for my t

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "From the man bringing his bundle of dried flesh for my teaching upwards, I have never refused instruction to any one."

items.name: The Master: I do not open up the truth to one who is not eager to ge

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I do not open up the truth to one who is not eager to get knowledge, nor help out any one who is not anxious to explain himself. When I have presented one corner of a subject to any one, and he cannot from it learn the other three, I do not repeat my lesson."

items.name: When the Master was eating by the side of a mourner, he n

sections.Teaching: When the Master was eating by the side of a mourner, he never ate to the full.

items.name: He did not sing on the same day in which he had been weeping

sections.Teaching: He did not sing on the same day in which he had been weeping.

items.name: The Master: to Yen Yuan, "When called to office, to undertake its du

sections.Teaching: The Master said to Yen Yuan, "When called to office, to undertake its duties; when not so called, to he retired;-it is only I and you who have attained to this."

items.name: Tsze-lu: If you had the conduct of the armies of a great state, w

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu said, "If you had the conduct of the armies of a great state, whom would you have to act with you?"

items.name: The Master: I would not have him to act with me, who will unarmed at

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I would not have him to act with me, who will unarmed attack a tiger, or cross a river without a boat, dying without any regret. My associate must be the man who proceeds to action full of solicitude, who is fond of adjusting his plans, and then carries them into execution."

items.name: The Master: If the search for riches is sure to be successful, thoug

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If the search for riches is sure to be successful, though I should become a groom with whip in hand to get them, I will do so. As the search may not be successful, I will follow after that which I love."

items.name: The things in reference to which the Master exercised the

sections.Teaching: The things in reference to which the Master exercised the greatest caution were-fasting, war, and sickness.

items.name: When the Master was in Ch'i, he heard the Shao, and for t

sections.Teaching: When the Master was in Ch'i, he heard the Shao, and for three months did not know the taste of flesh. "I did not think'" he said, "that music could have been made so excellent as this."

items.name: Yen Yu: Is our Master for the ruler of Wei

sections.Teaching: Yen Yu said, "Is our Master for the ruler of Wei?" Tsze-kung said, "Oh! I will ask him."

items.name: He went in accordingly, and said, "What sort of men were

sections.Teaching: He went in accordingly, and said, "What sort of men were Po-i and Shu-ch'i?" "They were ancient worthies," said the Master. "Did they have any repinings because of their course?" The Master again replied, "They sought to act virtuously, and they did so; what was there for them to repine about?" On this, Tsze-kung went out and said, "Our Master is not for him."

items.name: The Master: With coarse rice to eat, with water to drink, and my ben

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "With coarse rice to eat, with water to drink, and my bended arm for a pillow;-I have still joy in the midst of these things. Riches and honors acquired by unrighteousness, are to me as a floating cloud."

items.name: The Master: If some years were added to my life, I would give fifty

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If some years were added to my life, I would give fifty to the study of the Yi, and then I might come to be without great faults."

items.name: The Master: s frequent themes of discourse were-the Odes, the Histor

sections.Teaching: The Master's frequent themes of discourse were-the Odes, the History, and the maintenance of the Rules of Propriety. On all these he frequently discoursed.

items.name: The Duke of Sheh asked Tsze-lu about Confucius, and Tsze-

sections.Teaching: The Duke of Sheh asked Tsze-lu about Confucius, and Tsze-lu did not answer him.

items.name: The Master: Why did you not say to him,-He is simply a man, who in h

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Why did you not say to him,-He is simply a man, who in his eager pursuit of knowledge forgets his food, who in the joy of its attainment forgets his sorrows, and who does not perceive that old age is coming on?"

items.name: The Master: I am not one who was born in the possession of knowledge

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I am not one who was born in the possession of knowledge; I am one who is fond of antiquity, and earnest in seeking it there."

items.name: The subjects on which the Master did not talk, were-extra

sections.Teaching: The subjects on which the Master did not talk, were-extraordinary things, feats of strength, disorder, and spiritual beings.

items.name: The Master: When I walk along with two others, they may serve me as

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When I walk along with two others, they may serve me as my teachers. I will select their good qualities and follow them, their bad qualities and avoid them."

items.name: The Master: Heaven produced the virtue that is in me

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Heaven produced the virtue that is in me. Hwan T'ui-what can he do to me?"

items.name: The Master: Do you think, my disciples, that I have any concealments

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Do you think, my disciples, that I have any concealments? I conceal nothing from you. There is nothing which I do that is not shown to you, my disciples; that is my way."

items.name: There were four things which the Master taught,-letters,

sections.Teaching: There were four things which the Master taught,-letters, ethics, devotion of soul, and truthfulness.

items.name: The Master: A sage it is not mine to see

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A sage it is not mine to see; could I see a man of real talent and virtue, that would satisfy me."

items.name: The Master: A good man it is not mine to see

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A good man it is not mine to see; could I see a man possessed of constancy, that would satisfy me. "Having not and yet affecting to have, empty and yet affecting to be full, straitened and yet affecting to be at ease:-it is difficult with such characteristics to have constancy."

items.name: The Master: angled,-but did not use a net

sections.Teaching: The Master angled,-but did not use a net. He shot,-but not at birds perching.

items.name: The Master: There may be those who act without knowing why

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There may be those who act without knowing why. I do not do so. Hearing much and selecting what is good and following it; seeing much and keeping it in memory: this is the second style of knowledge."

items.name: It was difficult to talk profitably and reputably with th

sections.Teaching: It was difficult to talk profitably and reputably with the people of Hu-hsiang, and a lad of that place having had an interview with the Master, the disciples doubted.

items.name: The Master: I admit people's approach to me without committing mysel

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I admit people's approach to me without committing myself as to what they may do when they have retired. Why must one be so severe? If a man purify himself to wait upon me, I receive him so purified, without guaranteeing his past conduct."

items.name: The Master: Is virtue a thing remote

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Is virtue a thing remote? I wish to be virtuous, and lo! virtue is at hand."

items.name: The minister of crime of Ch'an asked whether the duke Cha

sections.Teaching: The minister of crime of Ch'an asked whether the duke Chao knew propriety, and Confucius said, "He knew propriety."

items.name: Confucius: having retired, the minister bowed to Wu-ma Ch'i to come

sections.Teaching: Confucius having retired, the minister bowed to Wu-ma Ch'i to come forward, and said, "I have heard that the superior man is not a partisan. May the superior man be a partisan also? The prince married a daughter of the house of WU, of the same surname with himself, and called her,-'The elder Tsze of Wu.' If the prince knew propriety, who does not know it?"

items.name: Wu-ma Ch'i reported these remarks, and the Master said, "

sections.Teaching: Wu-ma Ch'i reported these remarks, and the Master said, "I am fortunate! If I have any errors, people are sure to know them."

items.name: When the Master was in company with a person who was sing

sections.Teaching: When the Master was in company with a person who was singing, if he sang well, he would make him repeat the song, while he accompanied it with his own voice.

items.name: The Master: In letters I am perhaps equal to other men, but the char

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In letters I am perhaps equal to other men, but the character of the superior man, carrying out in his conduct what he professes, is what I have not yet attained to."

items.name: The Master: The sage and the man of perfect virtue

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The sage and the man of perfect virtue;-how dare I rank myself with them? It may simply be said of me, that I strive to become such without satiety, and teach others without weariness." Kung-hsi Hwa said, "This is just what we, the disciples, cannot imitate you in."

items.name: The Master: being very sick, Tsze-lu asked leave to pray for him

sections.Teaching: The Master being very sick, Tsze-lu asked leave to pray for him. He said, "May such a thing be done?" Tsze-lu replied, "It may. In the Eulogies it is said, 'Prayer has been made for thee to the spirits of the upper and lower worlds.'" The Master said, "My praying has been for a long time."

items.name: The Master: Extravagance leads to insubordination, and parsimony to

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Extravagance leads to insubordination, and parsimony to meanness. It is better to be mean than to be insubordinate."

items.name: The Master: The superior man is satisfied and composed

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man is satisfied and composed; the mean man is always full of distress."

items.name: The Master: was mild, and yet dignified

sections.Teaching: The Master was mild, and yet dignified; majestic, and yet not fierce; respectful, and yet easy.

items.name: Book 7 — Shu Er (Transmitting)

sections.Brief: The Master's own character and habits of learning. Confucius describes himself as a transmitter rather than an innovator, tireless in study, and never satisfied with his own virtue.

items.name: The Master: T'ai-po may be said to have reached the highest point of

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "T'ai-po may be said to have reached the highest point of virtuous action. Thrice he declined the kingdom, and the people in ignorance of his motives could not express their approbation of his conduct."

items.name: The Master: Respectfulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Respectfulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes laborious bustle; carefulness, without the rules of propriety, becomes timidity; boldness, without the rules of propriety, becomes insubordination; straightforwardness, without the rules of propriety, becomes rudeness. "When those who are in high stations perform well all their duties to their relations, the people are aroused to virtue. When old friends are not neglected by them, the people are preserved from meanness."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: being ill, he cared to him the disciples of...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang being ill, he cared to him the disciples of his school, and said, "Uncover my feet, uncover my hands. It is said in the Book of Poetry, 'We should be apprehensive and cautious, as if on the brink of a deep gulf, as if treading on thin ice, I and so have I been. Now and hereafter, I know my escape from all injury to my person. O ye, my little children."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: being ill, Meng Chang went to ask how he was

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang being ill, Meng Chang went to ask how he was.

items.name: Tsang: to him, "When a bird is about to die, its notes are mour

sections.Teaching: Tsang said to him, "When a bird is about to die, its notes are mournful; when a man is about to die, his words are good. "There are three principles of conduct which the man of high rank should consider specially important:-that in his deportment and manner he keep from violence and heedlessness; that in regulating his countenance he keep near to sincerity; and that in his words and tones he keep far from lowness and impropriety. As to such matters as attending to the sacrificial vessels, there are the proper officers for them."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: Gifted with ability, and yet putting questions...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "Gifted with ability, and yet putting questions to those who were not so; possessed of much, and yet putting questions to those possessed of little; having, as though he had not; full, and yet counting himself as empty; offended against, and yet entering into no altercation; formerly I had a friend who pursued this style of conduct."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: Suppose that there is an individual who can be...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "Suppose that there is an individual who can be entrusted with the charge of a young orphan prince, and can be commissioned with authority over a state of a hundred li, and whom no emergency however great can drive from his principles:-is such a man a superior man? He is a superior man indeed."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: The officer may not be without breadth of mind...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "The officer may not be without breadth of mind and vigorous endurance. His burden is heavy and his course is long. "Perfect virtue is the burden which he considers it is his to sustain;-is it not heavy? Only with death does his course stop;-is it not long?

items.name: The Master: It is by the Odes that the mind is aroused

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is by the Odes that the mind is aroused. "It is by the Rules of Propriety that the character is established. "It is from Music that the finish is received."

items.name: The Master: The people may be made to follow a path of action, but t

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The people may be made to follow a path of action, but they may not be made to understand it."

items.name: The Master: The man who is fond of daring and is dissatisfied with p

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The man who is fond of daring and is dissatisfied with poverty, will proceed to insubordination. So will the man who is not virtuous, when you carry your dislike of him to an extreme."

items.name: The Master: Though a man have abilities as admirable as those of the

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Though a man have abilities as admirable as those of the Duke of Chau, yet if he be proud and niggardly, those other things are really not worth being looked at."

items.name: The Master: It is not easy to find a man who has learned for three y

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is not easy to find a man who has learned for three years without coming to be good."

items.name: The Master: With sincere faith he unites the love of learning

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "With sincere faith he unites the love of learning; holding firm to death, he is perfecting the excellence of his course. "Such an one will not enter a tottering state, nor dwell in a disorganized one. When right principles of government prevail in the kingdom, he will show himself; when they are prostrated, he will keep concealed. "When a country is well governed, poverty and a mean condition are things to be ashamed of. When a country is ill governed, riches and honor are things to be ashamed of."

items.name: The Master: He who is not in any particular office has nothing to do

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who is not in any particular office has nothing to do with plans for the administration of its duties."

items.name: The Master: When the music master Chih first entered on his office,

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When the music master Chih first entered on his office, the finish of the Kwan Tsu was magnificent;-how it filled the ears!"

items.name: The Master: Ardent and yet not upright, stupid and yet not attentive

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Ardent and yet not upright, stupid and yet not attentive; simple and yet not sincere:-such persons I do not understand."

items.name: The Master: Learn as if you could not reach your object, and were al

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Learn as if you could not reach your object, and were always fearing also lest you should lose it."

items.name: The Master: How majestic was the manner in which Shun and Yu held po

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "How majestic was the manner in which Shun and Yu held possession of the empire, as if it were nothing to them!

items.name: The Master: Great indeed was Yao as a sovereign

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Great indeed was Yao as a sovereign! How majestic was he! It is only Heaven that is grand, and only Yao corresponded to it. How vast was his virtue! The people could find no name for it. "How majestic was he in the works which he accomplished! How glorious in the elegant regulations which he instituted!"

items.name: Shun: had five ministers, and the empire was well governed

sections.Teaching: Shun had five ministers, and the empire was well governed.

items.name: King Wu said, "I have ten able ministers

sections.Teaching: King Wu said, "I have ten able ministers."

items.name: Confucius: Is not the saying that talents are difficult to find, true

sections.Teaching: Confucius said, "Is not the saying that talents are difficult to find, true? Only when the dynasties of T'ang and Yu met, were they more abundant than in this of Chau, yet there was a woman among them. The able ministers were no more than nine men. "King Wan possessed two of the three parts of the empire, and with those he served the dynasty of Yin. The virtue of the house of Chau may be said to have reached the highest point indeed."

items.name: The Master: I can find no flaw in the character of Yu

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I can find no flaw in the character of Yu. He used himself coarse food and drink, but displayed the utmost filial piety towards the spirits. His ordinary garments were poor, but he displayed the utmost elegance in his sacrificial cap and apron. He lived in a low, mean house, but expended all his strength on the ditches and water channels. I can find nothing like a flaw in Yu."

items.name: Book 8 — Tai Bo

sections.Brief: Reflections on the sage-kings of antiquity (Yao, Shun, Yu) and the greatness of T'ai-po. The importance of music, reverence, and the difficulty of finding the Mean.

items.name: The subjects of which the Master seldom spoke were-profit

sections.Teaching: The subjects of which the Master seldom spoke were-profitableness, and also the appointments of Heaven, and perfect virtue.

items.name: A man of the village of Ta-hsiang said, "Great indeed is

sections.Teaching: A man of the village of Ta-hsiang said, "Great indeed is the philosopher K'ung! His learning is extensive, and yet he does not render his name famous by any particular thing."

items.name: The Master: heard the observation, and said to his disciples, "What

sections.Teaching: The Master heard the observation, and said to his disciples, "What shall I practice? Shall I practice charioteering, or shall I practice archery? I will practice charioteering."

items.name: The Master: The linen cap is that prescribed by the rules of ceremon

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The linen cap is that prescribed by the rules of ceremony, but now a silk one is worn. It is economical, and I follow the common practice. "The rules of ceremony prescribe the bowing below the hall, but now the practice is to bow only after ascending it. That is arrogant. I continue to bow below the hall, though I oppose the common practice."

items.name: There were four things from which the Master was entirely

sections.Teaching: There were four things from which the Master was entirely free. He had no foregone conclusions, no arbitrary predeterminations, no obstinacy, and no egoism.

items.name: The Master: was put in fear in K'wang

sections.Teaching: The Master was put in fear in K'wang.

items.name: He said, "After the death of King Wan, was not the cause

sections.Teaching: He said, "After the death of King Wan, was not the cause of truth lodged here in me? "If Heaven had wished to let this cause of truth perish, then I, a future mortal! should not have got such a relation to that cause. While Heaven does not let the cause of truth perish, what can the people of K'wang do to me?"

items.name: A high officer asked Tsze-kung, saying, "May we not say t

sections.Teaching: A high officer asked Tsze-kung, saying, "May we not say that your Master is a sage? How various is his ability!"

items.name: Tsze-kung: Certainly Heaven has endowed him unlimitedly

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Certainly Heaven has endowed him unlimitedly. He is about a sage. And, moreover, his ability is various."

items.name: The Master: heard of the conversation and said, "Does the high offic

sections.Teaching: The Master heard of the conversation and said, "Does the high officer know me? When I was young, my condition was low, and I acquired my ability in many things, but they were mean matters. Must the superior man have such variety of ability? He does not need variety of ability. Lao said, "The Master said, 'Having no official employment, I acquired many arts.'"

items.name: The Master: Am I indeed possessed of knowledge

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Am I indeed possessed of knowledge? I am not knowing. But if a mean person, who appears quite empty-like, ask anything of me, I set it forth from one end to the other, and exhaust it."

items.name: The Master: The Fang bird does not come

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The Fang bird does not come; the river sends forth no map:-it is all over with me!"

items.name: When the Master saw a person in a mourning dress, or any
