sections.Teaching: When the Master saw a person in a mourning dress, or any one with the cap and upper and lower garments of full dress, or a blind person, on observing them approaching, though they were younger than himself, he would rise up, and if he had to pass by them, he would do so hastily.

items.name: Yen Yuan: , in admiration of the Master's doctrines, sighed and sa

sections.Teaching: Yen Yuan, in admiration of the Master's doctrines, sighed and said, "I looked up to them, and they seemed to become more high; I tried to penetrate them, and they seemed to become more firm; I looked at them before me, and suddenly they seemed to be behind. "The Master, by orderly method, skillfully leads men on. He enlarged my mind with learning, and taught me the restraints of propriety. "When I wish to give over the study of his doctrines, I cannot do so, and having exerted all my ability, there seems something to stand right up before me; but though I wish to follow and lay hold of it, I really find no way to do so."

items.name: The Master: being very ill, Tsze-lu wished the disciples to act as m

sections.Teaching: The Master being very ill, Tsze-lu wished the disciples to act as ministers to him.

items.name: During a remission of his illness, he said, "Long has the

sections.Teaching: During a remission of his illness, he said, "Long has the conduct of Yu been deceitful! By pretending to have ministers when I have them not, whom should I impose upon? Should I impose upon Heaven? "Moreover, than that I should die in the hands of ministers, is it not better that I should die in the hands of you, my disciples? And though I may not get a great burial, shall I die upon the road?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: There is a beautiful gem here

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "There is a beautiful gem here. Should I lay it up in a case and keep it? or should I seek for a good price and sell it?" The Master said, "Sell it! Sell it! But I would wait for one to offer the price."

items.name: The Master: was wishing to go and live among the nine wild tribes of

sections.Teaching: The Master was wishing to go and live among the nine wild tribes of the east.

items.name: Some one said, "They are rude

sections.Teaching: Some one said, "They are rude. How can you do such a thing?" The Master said, "If a superior man dwelt among them, what rudeness would there be?"

items.name: The Master: I returned from Wei to Lu, and then the music was reform

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I returned from Wei to Lu, and then the music was reformed, and the pieces in the Royal songs and Praise songs all found their proper places."

items.name: The Master: Abroad, to serve the high ministers and nobles

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Abroad, to serve the high ministers and nobles; at home, to serve one's father and elder brothers; in all duties to the dead, not to dare not to exert one's self; and not to be overcome of wine:-which one of these things do I attain to?"

items.name: The Master: standing by a stream, said, "It passes on just like this,

sections.Teaching: The Master standing by a stream, said, "It passes on just like this, not ceasing day or night!"

items.name: The Master: I have not seen one who loves virtue as he loves beauty

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I have not seen one who loves virtue as he loves beauty."

items.name: The Master: The prosecution of learning may be compared to what may

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The prosecution of learning may be compared to what may happen in raising a mound. If there want but one basket of earth to complete the work, and I stop, the stopping is my own work. It may be compared to throwing down the earth on the level ground. Though but one basketful is thrown at a time, the advancing with it my own going forward."

items.name: The Master: Never flagging when I set forth anything to him

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Never flagging when I set forth anything to him;-ah! that is Hui." The Master said of Yen Yuan, "Alas! I saw his constant advance. I never saw him stop in his progress."

items.name: The Master: There are cases in which the blade springs, but the plan

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There are cases in which the blade springs, but the plant does not go on to flower! There are cases where it flowers but fruit is not subsequently produced!"

items.name: The Master: A youth is to be regarded with respect

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A youth is to be regarded with respect. How do we know that his future will not be equal to our present? If he reach the age of forty or fifty, and has not made himself heard of, then indeed he will not be worth being regarded with respect."

items.name: The Master: Can men refuse to assent to the words of strict admonition

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Can men refuse to assent to the words of strict admonition? But it is reforming the conduct because of them which is valuable. Can men refuse to be pleased with words of gentle advice? But it is unfolding their aim which is valuable. If a man be pleased with these words, but does not unfold their aim, and assents to those, but does not reform his conduct, I can really do nothing with him."

items.name: The Master: Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles. Have no friends not equal to yourself. When you have faults, do not fear to abandon them."

items.name: The Master: The commander of the forces of a large state may be carr

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The commander of the forces of a large state may be carried off, but the will of even a common man cannot be taken from him."

items.name: The Master: Dressed himself in a tattered robe quilted with hemp, ye

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Dressed himself in a tattered robe quilted with hemp, yet standing by the side of men dressed in furs, and not ashamed;-ah! it is Yu who is equal to this! "He dislikes none, he covets nothing;-what can he do but what is good!"

items.name: Tsze-lu: kept continually repeating these words of the ode, when

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu kept continually repeating these words of the ode, when the Master said, "Those things are by no means sufficient to constitute perfect excellence."

items.name: The Master: When the year becomes cold, then we know how the pine an

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When the year becomes cold, then we know how the pine and the cypress are the last to lose their leaves."

items.name: The Master: The wise are free from perplexities

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The wise are free from perplexities; the virtuous from anxiety; and the bold from fear."

items.name: The Master: There are some with whom we may study in common, but we

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There are some with whom we may study in common, but we shall find them unable to go along with us to principles. Perhaps we may go on with them to principles, but we shall find them unable to get established in those along with us. Or if we may get so established along with them, we shall find them unable to weigh occurring events along with us." "How the flowers of the aspen-plum flutter and turn! Do I not think of you? But your house is distant."

items.name: The Master: It is the want of thought about it

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is the want of thought about it. How is it distant?"

items.name: Book 9 — Zi Han (The Master Seldom Spoke)

sections.Brief: Topics the Master seldom discussed — profit, fate, and perfect virtue — and further glimpses into his personal conduct, humility, and commitment to culture even in adversity.

items.name: Confucius: , in his village, looked simple and sincere, and as if he

sections.Teaching: Confucius, in his village, looked simple and sincere, and as if he were not able to speak.

items.name: When he was in the prince's ancestral temple, or in the c

sections.Teaching: When he was in the prince's ancestral temple, or in the court, he spoke minutely on every point, but cautiously.

items.name: When he was waiting at court, in speaking with the great

sections.Teaching: When he was waiting at court, in speaking with the great officers of the lower grade, he spoke freely, but in a straightforward manner; in speaking with those of the higher grade, he did so blandly, but precisely.

items.name: When the ruler was present, his manner displayed respectf

sections.Teaching: When the ruler was present, his manner displayed respectful uneasiness; it was grave, but self-possessed.

items.name: When the prince called him to employ him in the reception

sections.Teaching: When the prince called him to employ him in the reception of a visitor, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to move forward with difficulty.

items.name: He inclined himself to the other officers among whom he s

sections.Teaching: He inclined himself to the other officers among whom he stood, moving his left or right arm, as their position required, but keeping the skirts of his robe before and behind evenly adjusted.

items.name: He hastened forward, with his arms like the wings of a bird

sections.Teaching: He hastened forward, with his arms like the wings of a bird.

items.name: When the guest had retired, he would report to the prince

sections.Teaching: When the guest had retired, he would report to the prince, "The visitor is not turning round any more."

items.name: When he entered the palace gate, he seemed to bend his bo

sections.Teaching: When he entered the palace gate, he seemed to bend his body, as if it were not sufficient to admit him.

items.name: When he was standing, he did not occupy the middle of the

sections.Teaching: When he was standing, he did not occupy the middle of the gateway; when he passed in or out, he did not tread upon the threshold.

items.name: When he was passing the vacant place of the prince, his

sections.Teaching: When he was passing the vacant place of the prince, his countenance appeared to change, and his legs to bend under him, and his words came as if he hardly had breath to utter them.

items.name: He ascended the reception hall, holding up his robe with

sections.Teaching: He ascended the reception hall, holding up his robe with both his hands, and his body bent; holding in his breath also, as if he dared not breathe.

items.name: When he came out from the audience, as soon as he had des

sections.Teaching: When he came out from the audience, as soon as he had descended one step, he began to relax his countenance, and had a satisfied look. When he had got the bottom of the steps, he advanced rapidly to his place, with his arms like wings, and on occupying it, his manner still showed respectful uneasiness.

items.name: When he was carrying the scepter of his ruler, he seemed

sections.Teaching: When he was carrying the scepter of his ruler, he seemed to bend his body, as if he were not able to bear its weight. He did not hold it higher than the position of the hands in making a bow, nor lower than their position in giving anything to another. His countenance seemed to change, and look apprehensive, and he dragged his feet along as if they were held by something to the ground.

items.name: In presenting the presents with which he was charged, he

sections.Teaching: In presenting the presents with which he was charged, he wore a placid appearance.

items.name: At his private audience, he looked highly pleased

sections.Teaching: At his private audience, he looked highly pleased.

items.name: The superior man did not use a deep purple, or a puce col

sections.Teaching: The superior man did not use a deep purple, or a puce color, in the ornaments of his dress.

items.name: Even in his undress, he did not wear anything of a red or

sections.Teaching: Even in his undress, he did not wear anything of a red or reddish color.

items.name: In warm weather, he had a single garment either of coarse

sections.Teaching: In warm weather, he had a single garment either of coarse or fine texture, but he wore it displayed over an inner garment.

items.name: Over lamb's fur he wore a garment of black

sections.Teaching: Over lamb's fur he wore a garment of black; over fawn's fur one of white; and over fox's fur one of yellow.

items.name: The fur robe of his undress was long, with the right slee

sections.Teaching: The fur robe of his undress was long, with the right sleeve short.

items.name: He required his sleeping dress to be half as long again a

sections.Teaching: He required his sleeping dress to be half as long again as his body.

items.name: When staying at home, he used thick furs of the fox or th

sections.Teaching: When staying at home, he used thick furs of the fox or the badger.

items.name: When he put off mourning, he wore all the appendages of t

sections.Teaching: When he put off mourning, he wore all the appendages of the girdle.

items.name: His undergarment, except when it was required to be of th

sections.Teaching: His undergarment, except when it was required to be of the curtain shape, was made of silk cut narrow above and wide below.

items.name: He did not wear lamb's fur or a black cap on a visit of c

sections.Teaching: He did not wear lamb's fur or a black cap on a visit of condolence.

items.name: On the first day of the month he put on his court robes,

sections.Teaching: On the first day of the month he put on his court robes, and presented himself at court.

items.name: When fasting, he thought it necessary to have his clothes

sections.Teaching: When fasting, he thought it necessary to have his clothes brightly clean and made of linen cloth.

items.name: When fasting, he thought it necessary to change his food,

sections.Teaching: When fasting, he thought it necessary to change his food, and also to change the place where he commonly sat in the apartment.

items.name: He did not dislike to have his rice finely cleaned, nor t

sections.Teaching: He did not dislike to have his rice finely cleaned, nor to have his mince meat cut quite small.

items.name: He did not eat rice which had been injured by heat or dam

sections.Teaching: He did not eat rice which had been injured by heat or damp and turned sour, nor fish or flesh which was gone. He did not eat what was discolored, or what was of a bad flavor, nor anything which was ill-cooked, or was not in season.

items.name: He did not eat meat which was not cut properly, nor what

sections.Teaching: He did not eat meat which was not cut properly, nor what was served without its proper sauce.

items.name: Though there might be a large quantity of meat, he would

sections.Teaching: Though there might be a large quantity of meat, he would not allow what he took to exceed the due proportion for the rice. It was only in wine that he laid down no limit for himself, but he did not allow himself to be confused by it.

items.name: He did not partake of wine and dried meat bought in the m

sections.Teaching: He did not partake of wine and dried meat bought in the market.

items.name: He was never without ginger when he ate

sections.Teaching: He was never without ginger when he ate. He did not eat much.

items.name: When he had been assisting at the prince's sacrifice, he

sections.Teaching: When he had been assisting at the prince's sacrifice, he did not keep the flesh which he received overnight. The flesh of his family sacrifice he did not keep over three days. If kept over three days, people could not eat it.

items.name: When eating, he did not converse

sections.Teaching: When eating, he did not converse. When in bed, he did not speak.

items.name: Although his food might be coarse rice and vegetable soup

sections.Teaching: Although his food might be coarse rice and vegetable soup, he would offer a little of it in sacrifice with a grave, respectful air.

items.name: If his mat was not straight, he did not sit on it

sections.Teaching: If his mat was not straight, he did not sit on it.

items.name: When the villagers were drinking together, upon those who

sections.Teaching: When the villagers were drinking together, upon those who carried staffs going out, he also went out immediately after.

items.name: When the villagers were going through their ceremonies to

sections.Teaching: When the villagers were going through their ceremonies to drive away pestilential influences, he put on his court robes and stood on the eastern steps.

items.name: When he was sending complimentary inquiries to any one in

sections.Teaching: When he was sending complimentary inquiries to any one in another state, he bowed twice as he escorted the messenger away.

items.name: Chi K: ang having sent him a present of physic, he bowed and rec

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang having sent him a present of physic, he bowed and received it, saying, "I do not know it. I dare not taste it."

items.name: The stable being burned down, when he was at court, on hi

sections.Teaching: The stable being burned down, when he was at court, on his return he said, "Has any man been hurt?" He did not ask about the horses.

items.name: When the he would adjust his mat, first taste it, and the

sections.Teaching: When the he would adjust his mat, first taste it, and then give it away to others. When the prince sent him a gift of undressed meat, he would have it cooked, and offer it to the spirits of his ancestors. When the prince sent him a gift of a living animal, he would keep it alive.

items.name: When he was in attendance on the prince and joining in th

sections.Teaching: When he was in attendance on the prince and joining in the entertainment, the prince only sacrificed. He first tasted everything.

items.name: When he was ill and the prince came to visit him, he had

sections.Teaching: When he was ill and the prince came to visit him, he had his head to the east, made his court robes be spread over him, and drew his girdle across them.

items.name: When the prince's order called him, without waiting for h

sections.Teaching: When the prince's order called him, without waiting for his carriage to be yoked, he went at once.

items.name: When he entered the ancestral temple of the state, he ask

sections.Teaching: When he entered the ancestral temple of the state, he asked about everything.

items.name: When any of his friends died, if he had no relations offi

sections.Teaching: When any of his friends died, if he had no relations offices, he would say, "I will bury him."

items.name: When a friend sent him a present, though it might be a ca

sections.Teaching: When a friend sent him a present, though it might be a carriage and horses, he did not bow.

items.name: The only present for which he bowed was that of the flesh

sections.Teaching: The only present for which he bowed was that of the flesh of sacrifice.

items.name: In bed, he did not lie like a corpse

sections.Teaching: In bed, he did not lie like a corpse. At home, he did not put on any formal deportment.

items.name: When he saw any one in a mourning dress, though it might

sections.Teaching: When he saw any one in a mourning dress, though it might be an acquaintance, he would change countenance; when he saw any one wearing the cap of full dress, or a blind person, though he might be in his undress, he would salute him in a ceremonious manner.

items.name: To any person in mourning he bowed forward to the crossba

sections.Teaching: To any person in mourning he bowed forward to the crossbar of his carriage; he bowed in the same way to any one bearing the tables of population.

items.name: When he was at an entertainment where there was an abunda

sections.Teaching: When he was at an entertainment where there was an abundance of provisions set before him, he would change countenance and rise up.

items.name: On a sudden clap of thunder, or a violent wind, he would

sections.Teaching: On a sudden clap of thunder, or a violent wind, he would change countenance.

items.name: When he was about to mount his carriage, he would stand s

sections.Teaching: When he was about to mount his carriage, he would stand straight, holding the cord.

items.name: When he was in the carriage, he did not turn his head qui

sections.Teaching: When he was in the carriage, he did not turn his head quite round, he did not talk hastily, he did not point with his hands.

items.name: Seeing the countenance, it instantly rises

sections.Teaching: Seeing the countenance, it instantly rises. It flies round, and by and by settles.

items.name: The Master: There is the hen-pheasant on the hill bridge

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There is the hen-pheasant on the hill bridge. At its season! At its season!" Tsze-lu made a motion to it. Thrice it smelt him and then rose.

items.name: Book 10 — Xiang Dang (In the Village)

sections.Brief: A detailed portrait of Confucius's daily life: how he dressed, ate, sat, slept, and conducted himself at court and in the village. A chapter of practice rather than preaching.

items.name: The Master: The men of former times in the matters of ceremonies and

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The men of former times in the matters of ceremonies and music were rustics, it is said, while the men of these latter times, in ceremonies and music, are accomplished gentlemen. "If I have occasion to use those things, I follow the men of former times."

items.name: The Master: Of those who were with me in Ch'an and Ts'ai, there are

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Of those who were with me in Ch'an and Ts'ai, there are none to be found to enter my door."

items.name: Distinguished for their virtuous principles and practice,

sections.Teaching: Distinguished for their virtuous principles and practice, there were Yen Yuan, Min Tsze-ch'ien, Zan Po-niu, and Chung-kung; for their ability in speech, Tsai Wo and Tsze-kung; for their administrative talents, Zan Yu and Chi Lu; for their literary acquirements, Tsze-yu and Tsze-hsia.

items.name: The Master: Hui gives me no assistance

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Hui gives me no assistance. There is nothing that I say in which he does not delight."

items.name: The Master: Filial indeed is Min Tsze-ch'ien

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Filial indeed is Min Tsze-ch'ien! Other people say nothing of him different from the report of his parents and brothers."

items.name: Nan Yung was frequently repeating the lines about a white

sections.Teaching: Nan Yung was frequently repeating the lines about a white scepter stone. Confucius gave him the daughter of his elder brother to wife.

items.name: Chi K: ang asked which of the disciples loved to learn

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang asked which of the disciples loved to learn. Confucius replied to him, "There was Yen Hui; he loved to learn. Unfortunately his appointed time was short, and he died. Now there is no one who loves to learn, as he did."

items.name: When Yen Yuan died, Yen Lu begged the carriage of the Mas

sections.Teaching: When Yen Yuan died, Yen Lu begged the carriage of the Master to sell and get an outer shell for his son's coffin.

items.name: The Master: Every one calls his son his son, whether he has talents

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Every one calls his son his son, whether he has talents or has not talents. There was Li; when he died, he had a coffin but no outer shell. I would not walk on foot to get a shell for him, because, having followed in the rear of the great officers, it was not proper that I should walk on foot."

items.name: When Yen Yuan died, the Master said, "Alas

sections.Teaching: When Yen Yuan died, the Master said, "Alas! Heaven is destroying me! Heaven is destroying me!"

items.name: When Yen Yuan died, the Master bewailed him exceedingly,

sections.Teaching: When Yen Yuan died, the Master bewailed him exceedingly, and the disciples who were with him said, "Master, your grief is excessive!" "Is it excessive?" said he. "If I am not to mourn bitterly for this man, for whom should I mourn?"

items.name: When Yen Yuan died, the disciples wished to give him a gr

sections.Teaching: When Yen Yuan died, the disciples wished to give him a great funeral, and the Master said, "You may not do so."

items.name: The disciples did bury him in great style

sections.Teaching: The disciples did bury him in great style.

items.name: The Master: Hui behaved towards me as his father

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Hui behaved towards me as his father. I have not been able to treat him as my son. The fault is not mine; it belongs to you, O disciples."

items.name: Chi Lu: serving the spirits of the dead

sections.Teaching: Chi Lu asked about serving the spirits of the dead. The Master said, "While you are not able to serve men, how can you serve their spirits?" Chi Lu added, "I venture to ask about death?" He was answered, "While you do not know life, how can you know about death?"

items.name: The disciple Min was standing by his side, looking bland

sections.Teaching: The disciple Min was standing by his side, looking bland and precise; Tsze-lu, looking bold and soldierly; Zan Yu and Tsze-kung, with a free and straightforward manner. The Master was pleased.

items.name: He said, "Yu, there

sections.Teaching: He said, "Yu, there!-he will not die a natural death."

items.name: Some parties in Lu were going to take down and rebuild th

sections.Teaching: Some parties in Lu were going to take down and rebuild the Long Treasury.

items.name: Min Tsze: -ch'ien said, "Suppose it were to be repaired after its

sections.Teaching: Min Tsze-ch'ien said, "Suppose it were to be repaired after its old style;-why must it be altered and made anew?"

items.name: The Master: This man seldom speaks

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "This man seldom speaks; when he does, he is sure to hit the point."

items.name: The Master: What has the lute of Yu to do in my door

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "What has the lute of Yu to do in my door?"

items.name: The other disciples began not to respect Tszelu

sections.Teaching: The other disciples began not to respect Tszelu. The Master said, "Yu has ascended to the hall, though he has not yet passed into the inner apartments."

items.name: Tsze-kung: of the two, Shih or Shang, was the superior

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked which of the two, Shih or Shang, was the superior. The Master said, "Shih goes beyond the due mean, and Shang does not come up to it." "Then," said Tsze-kung, "the superiority is with Shih, I suppose."

items.name: The Master: To go beyond is as wrong as to fall short

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "To go beyond is as wrong as to fall short."

items.name: The head of the Chi family was richer than the duke of Ch

sections.Teaching: The head of the Chi family was richer than the duke of Chau had been, and yet Ch'iu collected his imposts for him, and increased his wealth.

items.name: The Master: He is no disciple of mine

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He is no disciple of mine. My little children, beat the drum and assail him."

items.name: Ch'ai is simple

sections.Teaching: Ch'ai is simple. Shan is dull. Shih is specious. Yu is coarse.

items.name: The Master: There is Hui

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "There is Hui! He has nearly attained to perfect virtue. He is often in want. "Ts'ze does not acquiesce in the appointments of Heaven, and his goods are increased by him. Yet his judgments are often correct."

items.name: Tsze-chang: were the characteristics of the good man

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked what were the characteristics of the good man. The Master said, "He does not tread in the footsteps of others, but moreover, he does not enter the chamber of the sage."

items.name: The Master: If, because a man's discourse appears solid and sincere,

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If, because a man's discourse appears solid and sincere, we allow him to be a good man, is he really a superior man? or is his gravity only in appearance?"

items.name: Tsze-lu: he should immediately carry into practice what he heard

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu asked whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard. The Master said, "There are your father and elder brothers to be consulted;-why should you act on that principle of immediately carrying into practice what you hear?" Zan Yu asked the same, whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard, and the Master answered, "Immediately carry into practice what you hear." Kung-hsi Hwa said, "Yu asked whether he should carry immediately into practice what he heard, and you said, 'There are your father and elder brothers to be consulted.' Ch'iu asked whether he should immediately carry into practice what he heard, and you said, 'Carry it immediately into practice.' I, Ch'ih, am perplexed, and venture to ask you for an explanation." The Master said, "Ch'iu is retiring and slow; therefore I urged him forward. Yu has more than his own share of energy; therefore I kept him back."

items.name: The Master: was put in fear in K'wang and Yen Yuan fell behind

sections.Teaching: The Master was put in fear in K'wang and Yen Yuan fell behind. The Master, on his rejoining him, said, "I thought you had died." Hui replied, "While you were alive, how should I presume to die?"

items.name: Chi Tsze: -zan asked whether Chung Yu and Zan Ch'iu could be called

sections.Teaching: Chi Tsze-zan asked whether Chung Yu and Zan Ch'iu could be called great ministers.

items.name: The Master: I thought you would ask about some extraordinary individ

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I thought you would ask about some extraordinary individuals, and you only ask about Yu and Ch'iu! "What is called a great minister, is one who serves his prince according to what is right, and when he finds he cannot do so, retires. "Now, as to Yu and Ch'iu, they may be called ordinary ministers."

items.name: Tsze-zan: Then they will always follow their chief

sections.Teaching: Tsze-zan said, "Then they will always follow their chief;-win they?"

items.name: The Master: In an act of parricide or regicide, they would not follo

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In an act of parricide or regicide, they would not follow him."

items.name: Tsze-lu: got Tsze-kao appointed governor of Pi

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu got Tsze-kao appointed governor of Pi.

items.name: The Master: You are injuring a man's son

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "You are injuring a man's son."

items.name: Tsze-lu: There are, there, common people and officers

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu said, "There are, there, common people and officers; there are the altars of the spirits of the land and grain. Why must one read books before he can be considered to have learned?"

items.name: The Master: It is on this account that I hate your glib-tongued people

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "It is on this account that I hate your glib-tongued people."

items.name: Tsze-lu: , Tsang Hsi, Zan Yu, and Kunghsi Hwa were sitting by the

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu, Tsang Hsi, Zan Yu, and Kunghsi Hwa were sitting by the Master.

items.name: He said to them, "Though I am a day or so older than you,

sections.Teaching: He said to them, "Though I am a day or so older than you, do not think of that. "From day to day you are saying, 'We are not known.' If some ruler were to know you, what would you like to do?"

items.name: Tsze-lu: hastily and lightly replied, "Suppose the case of a state

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu hastily and lightly replied, "Suppose the case of a state of ten thousand chariots; let it be straitened between other large cities; let it be suffering from invading armies; and to this let there be added a famine in corn and in all vegetables:-if I were intrusted with the government of it, in three years' time I could make the people to be bold, and to recognize the rules of righteous conduct." The Master smiled at him.

items.name: Turning to Yen Yu, he said, "Ch'iu, what are your wishes

sections.Teaching: Turning to Yen Yu, he said, "Ch'iu, what are your wishes?" Ch'iu replied, "Suppose a state of sixty or seventy li square, or one of fifty or sixty, and let me have the government of it;-in three years' time, I could make plenty to abound among the people. As to teaching them the principles of propriety, and music, I must wait for the rise of a superior man to do that." "What are your wishes, Ch'ih," said the Master next to Kung-hsi Hwa. Ch'ih replied, "I do not say that my ability extends to these things, but I should wish to learn them. At the services of the ancestral temple, and at the audiences of the princes with the sovereign, I should like, dressed in the dark square-made robe and the black linen cap, to act as a small assistant."

items.name: Last of all, the Master asked Tsang Hsi, "Tien, what are

sections.Teaching: Last of all, the Master asked Tsang Hsi, "Tien, what are your wishes?" Tien, pausing as he was playing on his lute, while it was yet twanging, laid the instrument aside, and "My wishes," he said, "are different from the cherished purposes of these three gentlemen." "What harm is there in that?" said the Master; "do you also, as well as they, speak out your wishes." Tien then said, "In this, the last month of spring, with the dress of the season all complete, along with five or six young men who have assumed the cap, and six or seven boys, I would wash in the I, enjoy the breeze among the rain altars, and return home singing." The Master heaved a sigh and said, "I give my approval to Tien."

items.name: The three others having gone out, Tsang Hsi remained behi

sections.Teaching: The three others having gone out, Tsang Hsi remained behind, and said, "What do you think of the words of these three friends?" The Master replied, "They simply told each one his wishes."

items.name: Hsi pursued, "Master, why did you smile at Yu

sections.Teaching: Hsi pursued, "Master, why did you smile at Yu?"

items.name: He was answered, "The management of a state demands the r

sections.Teaching: He was answered, "The management of a state demands the rules of propriety. His words were not humble; therefore I smiled at him."

items.name: Hsi again said, "But was it not a state which Ch'iu propo

sections.Teaching: Hsi again said, "But was it not a state which Ch'iu proposed for himself?" The reply was, "Yes; did you ever see a territory of sixty or seventy li or one of fifty or sixty, which was not a state?"

items.name: Once more, Hsi inquired, "And was it not a state which Ch

sections.Teaching: Once more, Hsi inquired, "And was it not a state which Ch'ih proposed for himself?" The Master again replied, "Yes; who but princes have to do with ancestral temples, and with audiences but the sovereign? If Ch'ih were to be a small assistant in these services, who could be a great one?

items.name: Book 11 — Xian Jin (The Earlier Men)

sections.Brief: Comparisons between earlier and later generations of disciples. Confucius expresses preference for the earnest simplicity of the first disciples over the polished refinement of later ones.

items.name: Yen Yuan: perfect virtue

sections.Teaching: Yen Yuan asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, "To subdue one's self and return to propriety, is perfect virtue. If a man can for one day subdue himself and return to propriety, an under heaven will ascribe perfect virtue to him. Is the practice of perfect virtue from a man himself, or is it from others?"

items.name: Yen Yuan: I beg to ask the steps of that process

sections.Teaching: Yen Yuan said, "I beg to ask the steps of that process." The Master replied, "Look not at what is contrary to propriety; listen not to what is contrary to propriety; speak not what is contrary to propriety; make no movement which is contrary to propriety." Yen Yuan then said, "Though I am deficient in intelligence and vigor, I will make it my business to practice this lesson."

items.name: Chung-kung: perfect virtue

sections.Teaching: Chung-kung asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, "It is, when you go abroad, to behave to every one as if you were receiving a great guest; to employ the people as if you were assisting at a great sacrifice; not to do to others as you would not wish done to yourself; to have no murmuring against you in the country, and none in the family." Chung-kung said, "Though I am deficient in intelligence and vigor, I will make it my business to practice this lesson."

items.name: Sze-ma Niu asked about perfect virtue

sections.Teaching: Sze-ma Niu asked about perfect virtue.

items.name: The Master: The man of perfect virtue is cautious and slow in his sp

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The man of perfect virtue is cautious and slow in his speech." "Cautious and slow in his speech!" said Niu;-"is this what is meant by perfect virtue?" The Master said, "When a man feels the difficulty of doing, can he be other than cautious and slow in speaking?"

items.name: Sze-ma Niu asked about the superior man

sections.Teaching: Sze-ma Niu asked about the superior man. The Master said, "The superior man has neither anxiety nor fear." "Being without anxiety or fear!" said Nui;"does this constitute what we call the superior man?"

items.name: The Master: When internal examination discovers nothing wrong, what

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When internal examination discovers nothing wrong, what is there to be anxious about, what is there to fear?"

items.name: Sze-ma Niu, full of anxiety, said, "Other men all have th

sections.Teaching: Sze-ma Niu, full of anxiety, said, "Other men all have their brothers, I only have not."

items.name: Tsze-hsia: to him, "There is the following saying which I have hear

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia said to him, "There is the following saying which I have heard-'Death and life have their determined appointment; riches and honors depend upon Heaven.' "Let the superior man never fail reverentially to order his own conduct, and let him be respectful to others and observant of propriety:-then all within the four seas will be his brothers. What has the superior man to do with being distressed because he has no brothers?"

items.name: Tsze-chang: constituted intelligence

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked what constituted intelligence. The Master said, "He with whom neither slander that gradually soaks into the mind, nor statements that startle like a wound in the flesh, are successful may be called intelligent indeed. Yea, he with whom neither soaking slander, nor startling statements, are successful, may be called farseeing."

items.name: Tsze-kung: government

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked about government. The Master said, "The requisites of government are that there be sufficiency of food, sufficiency of military equipment, and the confidence of the people in their ruler."

items.name: Tsze-kung: If it cannot be helped, and one of these must be dispens

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "If it cannot be helped, and one of these must be dispensed with, which of the three should be foregone first?" "The military equipment," said the Master.

items.name: Tsze-kung: again asked, "If it cannot be helped, and one of the rem

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung again asked, "If it cannot be helped, and one of the remaining two must be dispensed with, which of them should be foregone?" The Master answered, "Part with the food. From of old, death has been the lot of an men; but if the people have no faith in their rulers, there is no standing for the state."

items.name: Chi Tsze: -ch'ang said, "In a superior man it is only the substant

sections.Teaching: Chi Tsze-ch'ang said, "In a superior man it is only the substantial qualities which are wanted;-why should we seek for ornamental accomplishments?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: Alas

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Alas! Your words, sir, show you to be a superior man, but four horses cannot overtake the tongue. Ornament is as substance; substance is as ornament. The hide of a tiger or a leopard stripped of its hair, is like the hide of a dog or a goat stripped of its hair."

items.name: The Duke Ai inquired of Yu Zo, saying, "The year is one o

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ai inquired of Yu Zo, saying, "The year is one of scarcity, and the returns for expenditure are not sufficient;-what is to be done?"

items.name: Yu: Zo replied to him, "Why not simply tithe the people

sections.Teaching: Yu Zo replied to him, "Why not simply tithe the people?" "With two tenths, said the duke, "I find it not enough;-how could I do with that system of one tenth?"

items.name: Yu: Zo answered, "If the people have plenty, their prince wil

sections.Teaching: Yu Zo answered, "If the people have plenty, their prince will not be left to want alone. If the people are in want, their prince cannot enjoy plenty alone."

items.name: Tsze-chang: having asked how virtue was to be exalted, and delusions

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang having asked how virtue was to be exalted, and delusions to be discovered, the Master said, "Hold faithfulness and sincerity as first principles, and be moving continually to what is right,-this is the way to exalt one's virtue. "You love a man and wish him to live; you hate him and wish him to die. Having wished him to live, you also wish him to die. This is a case of delusion. 'It may not be on account of her being rich, yet you come to make a difference.'"

items.name: The Duke Ching, of Ch'i, asked Confucius about government

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ching, of Ch'i, asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, "There is government, when the prince is prince, and the minister is minister; when the father is father, and the son is son." "Good!" said the duke; "if, indeed, the prince be not prince, the not minister, the father not father, and the son not son, although I have my revenue, can I enjoy it?"

items.name: The Master: Ah

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Ah! it is Yu, who could with half a word settle litigations!"

items.name: Tsze-lu: never slept over a promise

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu never slept over a promise.

items.name: The Master: In hearing litigations, I am like any other body

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In hearing litigations, I am like any other body. What is necessary, however, is to cause the people to have no litigations."

items.name: Tsze-chang: government

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked about government. The Master said, "The art of governing is to keep its affairs before the mind without weariness, and to practice them with undeviating consistency."

items.name: The Master: By extensively studying all learning, and keeping himsel

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "By extensively studying all learning, and keeping himself under the restraint of the rules of propriety, one may thus likewise not err from what is right."

items.name: The Master: The superior man seeks to perfect the admirable qualitie

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man seeks to perfect the admirable qualities of men, and does not seek to perfect their bad qualities. The mean man does the opposite of this."

items.name: Chi K: ang asked Confucius about government

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang asked Confucius about government. Confucius replied, "To govern means to rectify. If you lead on the people with correctness, who will dare not to be correct?"

items.name: Chi K: ang, distressed about the number of thieves in the state,

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang, distressed about the number of thieves in the state, inquired of Confucius how to do away with them. Confucius said, "If you, sir, were not covetous, although you should reward them to do it, they would not steal."

items.name: Chi K: ang asked Confucius about government, saying, "What do yo

sections.Teaching: Chi K'ang asked Confucius about government, saying, "What do you say to killing the unprincipled for the good of the principled?" Confucius replied, "Sir, in carrying on your government, why should you use killing at all? Let your evinced desires be for what is good, and the people will be good. The relation between superiors and inferiors is like that between the wind and the grass. The grass must bend, when the wind blows across it."

items.name: Tsze-chang: asked, "What must the officer be, who may be said to be

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang asked, "What must the officer be, who may be said to be distinguished?"

items.name: The Master: What is it you call being distinguished

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "What is it you call being distinguished?"

items.name: Tsze-chang: replied, "It is to be heard of through the state, to be

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang replied, "It is to be heard of through the state, to be heard of throughout his clan."

items.name: The Master: That is notoriety, not distinction

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "That is notoriety, not distinction. "Now the man of distinction is solid and straightforward, and loves righteousness. He examines people's words, and looks at their countenances. He is anxious to humble himself to others. Such a man will be distinguished in the country; he will be distinguished in his clan. "As to the man of notoriety, he assumes the appearance of virtue, but his actions are opposed to it, and he rests in this character without any doubts about himself. Such a man will be heard of in the country; he will be heard of in the clan."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: rambling with the Master under the trees about the rain

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih rambling with the Master under the trees about the rain altars, said, "I venture to ask how to exalt virtue, to correct cherished evil, and to discover delusions."

items.name: The Master: Truly a good question

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Truly a good question! "If doing what is to be done be made the first business, and success a secondary consideration:-is not this the way to exalt virtue? To assail one's own wickedness and not assail that of others;-is not this the way to correct cherished evil? For a morning's anger to disregard one's own life, and involve that of his parents;-is not this a case of delusion?"

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: benevolence

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih asked about benevolence. The Master said, "It is to love all men." He asked about knowledge. The Master said, "It is to know all men."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: did not immediately understand these answers

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih did not immediately understand these answers.

items.name: The Master: Employ the upright and put aside all the crooked

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Employ the upright and put aside all the crooked; in this way the crooked can be made to be upright."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: retired, and, seeing Tsze-hsia, he said to him, "A Littl

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih retired, and, seeing Tsze-hsia, he said to him, "A Little while ago, I had an interview with our Master, and asked him about knowledge. He said, 'Employ the upright, and put aside all the crooked;-in this way, the crooked will be made to be upright.' What did he mean?"

items.name: Tsze-hsia: Truly rich is his saying

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia said, "Truly rich is his saying! "Shun, being in possession of the kingdom, selected from among all the people, and employed Kai-yao-on which all who were devoid of virtue disappeared. T'ang, being in possession of the kingdom, selected from among all the people, and employed I Yin-and an who were devoid of virtue disappeared."

items.name: Tsze-kung: friendship

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked about friendship. The Master said, "Faithfully admonish your friend, and skillfully lead him on. If you find him impracticable, stop. Do not disgrace yourself."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: The superior man on grounds of culture meets...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "The superior man on grounds of culture meets with his friends, and by friendship helps his virtue."

items.name: Book 12 — Yan Yuan

sections.Brief: The central virtue of ren (benevolence) explored in dialogue. Yan Yuan, Zhong Gong, and others ask about ren and receive answers tailored to their individual characters and situations.

items.name: Tsze-lu: government

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu asked about government. The Master said, "Go before the people with your example, and be laborious in their affairs."

items.name: He requested further instruction, and was answered, "Be n

sections.Teaching: He requested further instruction, and was answered, "Be not weary in these things."

items.name: Chung-kung: , being chief minister to the head of the Chi family, as

sections.Teaching: Chung-kung, being chief minister to the head of the Chi family, asked about government. The Master said, "Employ first the services of your various officers, pardon small faults, and raise to office men of virtue and talents."

items.name: Chung-kung: How shall I know the men of virtue and talent, so that I

sections.Teaching: Chung-kung said, "How shall I know the men of virtue and talent, so that I may raise them to office?" He was answered, "Raise to office those whom you know. As to those whom you do not know, will others neglect them?"

items.name: Tsze-lu: The ruler of Wei has been waiting for you, in order with

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu said, "The ruler of Wei has been waiting for you, in order with you to administer the government. What will you consider the first thing to be done?"

items.name: The Master: replied, "What is necessary is to rectify names

sections.Teaching: The Master replied, "What is necessary is to rectify names." "So! indeed!" said Tsze-lu. "You are wide of the mark! Why must there be such rectification?"

items.name: The Master: How uncultivated you are, Yu

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "How uncultivated you are, Yu! A superior man, in regard to what he does not know, shows a cautious reserve. "If names be not correct, language is not in accordance with the truth of things. If language be not in accordance with the truth of things, affairs cannot be carried on to success. "When affairs cannot be carried on to success, proprieties and music do not flourish. When proprieties and music do not flourish, punishments will not be properly awarded. When punishments are not properly awarded, the people do not know how to move hand or foot. "Therefore a superior man considers it necessary that the names he uses may be spoken appropriately, and also that what he speaks may be carried out appropriately. What the superior man requires is just that in his words there may be nothing incorrect."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: requested to be taught husbandry

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih requested to be taught husbandry. The Master said, "I am not so good for that as an old husbandman." He requested also to be taught gardening, and was answered, "I am not so good for that as an old gardener."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: having gone out, the Master said, "A small man, indeed, i

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih having gone out, the Master said, "A small man, indeed, is Fan Hsu! If a superior man love propriety, the people will not dare not to be reverent. If he love righteousness, the people will not dare not to submit to his example. If he love good faith, the people will not dare not to be sincere. Now, when these things obtain, the people from all quarters will come to him, bearing their children on their backs; what need has he of a knowledge of husbandry?"

items.name: The Master: Though a man may be able to recite the three hundred ode

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Though a man may be able to recite the three hundred odes, yet if, when intrusted with a governmental charge, he knows not how to act, or if, when sent to any quarter on a mission, he cannot give his replies unassisted, notwithstanding the extent of his learning, of what practical use is it?"

items.name: The Master: When a prince's personal conduct is correct, his governm

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When a prince's personal conduct is correct, his government is effective without the issuing of orders. If his personal conduct is not correct, he may issue orders, but they will not be followed."

items.name: The Master: The governments of Lu and Wei are brothers

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The governments of Lu and Wei are brothers."

items.name: The Master: of Ching, a scion of the ducal family of Wei, that he kn

sections.Teaching: The Master said of Ching, a scion of the ducal family of Wei, that he knew the economy of a family well. When he began to have means, he said, "Ha! here is a collection-!" When they were a little increased, he said, "Ha! this is complete!" When he had become rich, he said, "Ha! this is admirable!"

items.name: When the Master went to Weil Zan Yu acted as driver of hi

sections.Teaching: When the Master went to Weil Zan Yu acted as driver of his carriage.

items.name: The Master: observed, "How numerous are the people

sections.Teaching: The Master observed, "How numerous are the people!"

items.name: Yu: Since they are thus numerous, what more shall be done fo

sections.Teaching: Yu said, "Since they are thus numerous, what more shall be done for them?" "Enrich them, was the reply. "And when they have been enriched, what more shall be done?" The Master said, "Teach them."

items.name: The Master: If there were any of the princes who would employ me, in

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If there were any of the princes who would employ me, in the course of twelve months, I should have done something considerable. In three years, the government would be perfected."

items.name: The Master: If good men were to govern a country in succession for a

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "'If good men were to govern a country in succession for a hundred years, they would be able to transform the violently bad, and dispense with capital punishments.' True indeed is this saying!"

items.name: The Master: If a truly royal ruler were to arise, it would stir requ

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a truly royal ruler were to arise, it would stir require a generation, and then virtue would prevail."

items.name: The Master: If a minister make his own conduct correct, what difficu

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If a minister make his own conduct correct, what difficulty will he have in assisting in government? If he cannot rectify himself, what has he to do with rectifying others?"

items.name: The disciple Zan returning from the court, the Master sai

sections.Teaching: The disciple Zan returning from the court, the Master said to him, "How are you so late?" He replied, "We had government business." The Master said, "It must have been family affairs. If there had been government business, though I am not now in office, I should have been consulted about it."

items.name: The Duke Ting asked whether there was a single sentence w

sections.Teaching: The Duke Ting asked whether there was a single sentence which could make a country prosperous. Confucius replied, "Such an effect cannot be expected from one sentence. "There is a saying, however, which people have -'To be a prince is difficult; to be a minister is not easy.' "If a ruler knows this,-the difficulty of being a prince,-may there not be expected from this one sentence the prosperity of his country?"

items.name: The duke then said, "Is there a single sentence which can

sections.Teaching: The duke then said, "Is there a single sentence which can ruin a country?" Confucius replied, "Such an effect as that cannot be expected from one sentence. There is, however, the saying which people have-'I have no pleasure in being a prince, but only in that no one can offer any opposition to what I say!' "If a ruler's words be good, is it not also good that no one oppose them? But if they are not good, and no one opposes them, may there not be expected from this one sentence the ruin of his country?"

items.name: The Duke of Sheh asked about government

sections.Teaching: The Duke of Sheh asked about government.

items.name: The Master: Good government obtains when those who are near are made

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Good government obtains when those who are near are made happy, and those who are far off are attracted."

items.name: Passage 26

sections.Teaching: Tsze-hsia! being governor of Chu-fu, asked about government. The Master said, "Do not be desirous to have things done quickly; do not look at small advantages. Desire to have things done quickly prevents their being done thoroughly. Looking at small advantages prevents great affairs from being accomplished."

items.name: The Duke of Sheh informed Confucius, saying, "Among us he

sections.Teaching: The Duke of Sheh informed Confucius, saying, "Among us here there are those who may be styled upright in their conduct. If their father have stolen a sheep, they will bear witness to the fact."

items.name: Confucius: Among us, in our part of the country, those who are upri

sections.Teaching: Confucius said, "Among us, in our part of the country, those who are upright are different from this. The father conceals the misconduct of the son, and the son conceals the misconduct of the father. Uprightness is to be found in this."

items.name: Fan Ch'ih: perfect virtue

sections.Teaching: Fan Ch'ih asked about perfect virtue. The Master said, "It is, in retirement, to be sedately grave; in the management of business, to be reverently attentive; in intercourse with others, to be strictly sincere. Though a man go among rude, uncultivated tribes, these qualities may not be neglected."

items.name: Tsze-kung: asked, saying, "What qualities must a man possess to ent

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked, saying, "What qualities must a man possess to entitle him to be called an officer? The Master said, "He who in his conduct of himself maintains a sense of shame, and when sent to any quarter will not disgrace his prince's commission, deserves to be called an officer."

items.name: Tsze-kung: pursued, "I venture to ask who may be placed in the next

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung pursued, "I venture to ask who may be placed in the next lower rank?" And he was told, "He whom the circle of his relatives pronounce to be filial, whom his fellow villagers and neighbors pronounce to be fraternal."

items.name: Again the disciple asked, "I venture to ask about the cla

sections.Teaching: Again the disciple asked, "I venture to ask about the class still next in order." The Master said, "They are determined to be sincere in what they say, and to carry out what they do. They are obstinate little men. Yet perhaps they may make the next class."

items.name: Tsze-kung: finally inquired, "Of what sort are those of the present

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung finally inquired, "Of what sort are those of the present day, who engage in government?" The Master said "Pooh! they are so many pecks and hampers, not worth being taken into account."

items.name: The Master: Since I cannot get men pursuing the due medium, to whom

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Since I cannot get men pursuing the due medium, to whom I might communicate my instructions, I must find the ardent and the cautiously-decided. The ardent will advance and lay hold of truth; the cautiously-decided will keep themselves from what is wrong."

items.name: The Master: The people of the south have a saying -'A man without co

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The people of the south have a saying -'A man without constancy cannot be either a wizard or a doctor.' Good! "Inconstant in his virtue, he will be visited with disgrace."

items.name: The Master: This arises simply from not attending to the prognostica

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "This arises simply from not attending to the prognostication."

items.name: The Master: The superior man is affable, but not adulatory

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man is affable, but not adulatory; the mean man is adulatory, but not affable."

items.name: Tsze-kung: asked, saying, "What do you say of a man who is loved by

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung asked, saying, "What do you say of a man who is loved by all the people of his neighborhood?" The Master replied, "We may not for that accord our approval of him." "And what do you say of him who is hated by all the people of his neighborhood?" The Master said, "We may not for that conclude that he is bad. It is better than either of these cases that the good in the neighborhood love him, and the bad hate him."

items.name: The Master: The superior man is easy to serve and difficult to please

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man is easy to serve and difficult to please. If you try to please him in any way which is not accordant with right, he will not be pleased. But in his employment of men, he uses them according to their capacity. The mean man is difficult to serve, and easy to please. If you try to please him, though it be in a way which is not accordant with right, he may be pleased. But in his employment of men, he wishes them to be equal to everything."

items.name: The Master: The superior man has a dignified ease without pride

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man has a dignified ease without pride. The mean man has pride without a dignified ease."

items.name: The Master: The firm, the enduring, the simple, and the modest are n

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The firm, the enduring, the simple, and the modest are near to virtue."

items.name: Tsze-lu: asked, saying, "What qualities must a man possess to enti

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu asked, saying, "What qualities must a man possess to entitle him to be called a scholar?" The Master said, "He must be thus,-earnest, urgent, and bland:-among his friends, earnest and urgent; among his brethren, bland."

items.name: The Master: Let a good man teach the people seven years, and they ma

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Let a good man teach the people seven years, and they may then likewise be employed in war."

items.name: The Master: To lead an uninstructed people to war, is to throw them

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "To lead an uninstructed people to war, is to throw them away."

items.name: Book 13 — Zi Lu

sections.Brief: Practical governance: rectification of names, leading by example, and the relationship between personal virtue and political order. If names are not correct, language is confused and nothing can be accomplished.

items.name: Hsien asked what was shameful

sections.Teaching: Hsien asked what was shameful. The Master said, "When good government prevails in a state, to be thinking only of salary; and, when bad government prevails, to be thinking, in the same way, only of salary;-this is shameful." "When the love of superiority, boasting, resentments, and covetousness are repressed, this may be deemed perfect virtue."

items.name: The Master: This may be regarded as the achievement of what is diffi

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "This may be regarded as the achievement of what is difficult. But I do not know that it is to be deemed perfect virtue."

items.name: The Master: The scholar who cherishes the love of comfort is not fit

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The scholar who cherishes the love of comfort is not fit to be deemed a scholar."

items.name: The Master: When good government prevails in a state, language may b

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "When good government prevails in a state, language may be lofty and bold, and actions the same. When bad government prevails, the actions may be lofty and bold, but the language may be with some reserve."

items.name: The Master: The virtuous will be sure to speak correctly, but those

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The virtuous will be sure to speak correctly, but those whose speech is good may not always be virtuous. Men of principle are sure to be bold, but those who are bold may not always be men of principle."

items.name: Nan-kung Kwo: , submitting an inquiry to Confucius, said, "I was skill

sections.Teaching: Nan-kung Kwo, submitting an inquiry to Confucius, said, "I was skillful at archery, and Ao could move a boat along upon the land, but neither of them died a natural death. Yu and Chi personally wrought at the toils of husbandry, and they became possessors of the kingdom." The Master made no reply; but when Nan-kung Kwo went out, he said, "A superior man indeed is this! An esteemer of virtue indeed is this!"

items.name: The Master: Superior men, and yet not always virtuous, there have be

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Superior men, and yet not always virtuous, there have been, alas! But there never has been a mean man, and, at the same time, virtuous."

items.name: The Master: Can there be love which does not lead to strictness with

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Can there be love which does not lead to strictness with its object? Can there be loyalty which does not lead to the instruction of its object?"

items.name: The Master: In preparing the governmental notifications, P'i Shan fi

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In preparing the governmental notifications, P'i Shan first made the rough draft; Shi-shu examined and discussed its contents; Tsze-yu, the manager of foreign intercourse, then polished the style; and, finally, Tsze-ch'an of Tung-li gave it the proper elegance and finish."

items.name: Some one asked about Tsze-ch'an

sections.Teaching: Some one asked about Tsze-ch'an. The Master said, "He was a kind man."

items.name: He asked about Tsze-hsi

sections.Teaching: He asked about Tsze-hsi. The Master said, "That man! That man!"

items.name: He asked about Kwan Chung

sections.Teaching: He asked about Kwan Chung. "For him," said the Master, "the city of Pien, with three hundred families, was taken from the chief of the Po family, who did not utter a murmuring word, though, to the end of his life, he had only coarse rice to eat."

items.name: The Master: To be poor without murmuring is difficult

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "To be poor without murmuring is difficult. To be rich without being proud is easy."

items.name: The Master: Mang Kung-ch'o is more than fit to be chief officer in t

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Mang Kung-ch'o is more than fit to be chief officer in the families of Chao and Wei, but he is not fit to be great officer to either of the states Tang or Hsieh."

items.name: Tsze-lu: constituted a COMPLETE man

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu asked what constituted a COMPLETE man. The Master said, "Suppose a man with the knowledge of Tsang Wu-chung, the freedom from covetousness of Kung-ch'o, the bravery of Chwang of Pien, and the varied talents of Zan Ch'iu; add to these the accomplishments of the rules of propriety and music;-such a one might be reckoned a COMPLETE man."

items.name: He then added, "But what is the necessity for a complete

sections.Teaching: He then added, "But what is the necessity for a complete man of the present day to have all these things? The man, who in the view of gain, thinks of righteousness; who in the view of danger is prepared to give up his life; and who does not forget an old agreement however far back it extends:-such a man may be reckoned a COMPLETE man."

items.name: The Master: -ming Chia about Kung-shu Wan, saying, "Is it true that

sections.Teaching: The Master asked Kung-ming Chia about Kung-shu Wan, saying, "Is it true that your master speaks not, laughs not, and takes not?"

items.name: Kung-ming Chia replied, "This has arisen from the reporte

sections.Teaching: Kung-ming Chia replied, "This has arisen from the reporters going beyond the truth.-My master speaks when it is the time to speak, and so men do not get tired of his speaking. He laughs when there is occasion to be joyful, and so men do not get tired of his laughing. He takes when it is consistent with righteousness to do so, and so men do not get tired of his taking." The Master said, "So! But is it so with him?"

items.name: The Master: Tsang Wu-chung, keeping possession of Fang, asked of the

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Tsang Wu-chung, keeping possession of Fang, asked of the duke of Lu to appoint a successor to him in his family. Although it may be said that he was not using force with his sovereign, I believe he was."

items.name: The Master: The duke Wan of Tsin was crafty and not upright

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The duke Wan of Tsin was crafty and not upright. The duke Hwan of Ch'i was upright and not crafty."

items.name: Tsze-lu: The Duke Hwan caused his brother Chiu to be killed, when

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu said, "The Duke Hwan caused his brother Chiu to be killed, when Shao Hu died, with his master, but Kwan Chung did not die. May not I say that he was wanting in virtue?"

items.name: The Master: The Duke Hwan assembled all the princes together, and th

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The Duke Hwan assembled all the princes together, and that not with weapons of war and chariots:-it was all through the influence of Kwan Chung. Whose beneficence was like his? Whose beneficence was like his?"

items.name: Tsze-kung: Kwan Chung, I apprehend was wanting in virtue

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Kwan Chung, I apprehend was wanting in virtue. When the Duke Hwan caused his brother Chiu to be killed, Kwan Chung was not able to die with him. Moreover, he became prime minister to Hwan."

items.name: The Master: Kwan Chung acted as prime minister to the Duke Hwan made

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Kwan Chung acted as prime minister to the Duke Hwan made him leader of all the princes, and united and rectified the whole kingdom. Down to the present day, the people enjoy the gifts which he conferred. But for Kwan Chung, we should now be wearing our hair unbound, and the lappets of our coats buttoning on the left side. "Will you require from him the small fidelity of common men and common women, who would commit suicide in a stream or ditch, no one knowing anything about them?"

items.name: The great officer, Hsien, who had been family minister to

sections.Teaching: The great officer, Hsien, who had been family minister to Kung-shu Wan, ascended to the prince's court in company with Wan.

items.name: The Master: , having heard of it, said, "He deserved to be considere

sections.Teaching: The Master, having heard of it, said, "He deserved to be considered WAN (the accomplished)."

items.name: The Master: was speaking about the unprincipled course of the duke L

sections.Teaching: The Master was speaking about the unprincipled course of the duke Ling of Weil when Ch'i K'ang said, "Since he is of such a character, how is it he does not lose his state?"

items.name: Confucius: The Chung-shu Yu has the superintendence of his guests a

sections.Teaching: Confucius said, "The Chung-shu Yu has the superintendence of his guests and of strangers; the litanist, T'o, has the management of his ancestral temple; and Wang-sun Chia has the direction of the army and forces:-with such officers as these, how should he lose his state?"

items.name: The Master: He who speaks without modesty will find it difficult to

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who speaks without modesty will find it difficult to make his words good."

items.name: Chan Ch'ang murdered the Duke Chien of Ch'i

sections.Teaching: Chan Ch'ang murdered the Duke Chien of Ch'i.

items.name: Confucius: bathed, went to court and informed the Duke Ai, saying,

sections.Teaching: Confucius bathed, went to court and informed the Duke Ai, saying, "Chan Hang has slain his sovereign. I beg that you will undertake to punish him."

items.name: The duke said, "Inform the chiefs of the three families o

sections.Teaching: The duke said, "Inform the chiefs of the three families of it."

items.name: Confucius: retired, and said, "Following in the rear of the great o

sections.Teaching: Confucius retired, and said, "Following in the rear of the great officers, I did not dare not to represent such a matter, and my prince says, "Inform the chiefs of the three families of it."

items.name: He went to the chiefs, and informed them, but they would

sections.Teaching: He went to the chiefs, and informed them, but they would not act. Confucius then said, "Following in the rear of the great officers, I did not dare not to represent such a matter."

items.name: Tsze-lu: a ruler should be served

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu asked how a ruler should be served. The Master said, "Do not impose on him, and, moreover, withstand him to his face."

items.name: The Master: The progress of the superior man is upwards

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The progress of the superior man is upwards; the progress of the mean man is downwards."

items.name: The Master: In ancient times, men learned with a view to their own i

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "In ancient times, men learned with a view to their own improvement. Nowadays, men learn with a view to the approbation of others."

items.name: Chu Po-yu sent a messenger with friendly inquiries to Con

sections.Teaching: Chu Po-yu sent a messenger with friendly inquiries to Confucius.

items.name: Confucius: sat with him, and questioned him

sections.Teaching: Confucius sat with him, and questioned him. "What," said he! "is your master engaged in?" The messenger replied, "My master is anxious to make his faults few, but he has not yet succeeded." He then went out, and the Master said, "A messenger indeed! A messenger indeed!"

items.name: The Master: He who is not in any particular office has nothing to do

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who is not in any particular office has nothing to do with plans for the administration of its duties."

items.name: The philosopher Tsang: The superior man, in his thoughts, does not go...

sections.Teaching: The philosopher Tsang said, "The superior man, in his thoughts, does not go out of his place."

items.name: The Master: The superior man is modest in his speech, but exceeds in

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The superior man is modest in his speech, but exceeds in his actions."

items.name: The Master: The way of the superior man is threefold, but I am not e

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "The way of the superior man is threefold, but I am not equal to it. Virtuous, he is free from anxieties; wise, he is free from perplexities; bold, he is free from fear.

items.name: Tsze-kung: Master, that is what you yourself say

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "Master, that is what you yourself say."

items.name: Tsze-kung: was in the habit of comparing men together

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung was in the habit of comparing men together. The Master said, "Tsze must have reached a high pitch of excellence! Now, I have not leisure for this."

items.name: The Master: I will not be concerned at men's not knowing me

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "I will not be concerned at men's not knowing me; I will be concerned at my own want of ability."

items.name: The Master: He who does not anticipate attempts to deceive him, nor

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "He who does not anticipate attempts to deceive him, nor think beforehand of his not being believed, and yet apprehends these things readily when they occur;-is he not a man of superior worth?"

items.name: Wei-shang Mau said to Confucius, "Ch'iu, how is it that y

sections.Teaching: Wei-shang Mau said to Confucius, "Ch'iu, how is it that you keep roosting about? Is it not that you are an insinuating talker?

items.name: Confucius: I do not dare to play the part of such a talker, but I h

sections.Teaching: Confucius said, "I do not dare to play the part of such a talker, but I hate obstinacy."

items.name: The Master: A horse is called a ch'i, not because of its strength, b

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "A horse is called a ch'i, not because of its strength, but because of its other good qualities."

items.name: Some one said, "What do you say concerning the principle

sections.Teaching: Some one said, "What do you say concerning the principle that injury should be recompensed with kindness?"

items.name: The Master: With what then will you recompense kindness

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "With what then will you recompense kindness?" "Recompense injury with justice, and recompense kindness with kindness."

items.name: The Master: Alas

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Alas! there is no one that knows me."

items.name: Tsze-kung: What do you mean by thus saying-that no one knows you

sections.Teaching: Tsze-kung said, "What do you mean by thus saying-that no one knows you?" The Master replied, "I do not murmur against Heaven. I do not grumble against men. My studies lie low, and my penetration rises high. But there is Heaven;-that knows me!"

items.name: The Kung-po Liao, having slandered Tsze-lu to Chi-sun, Ts

sections.Teaching: The Kung-po Liao, having slandered Tsze-lu to Chi-sun, Tsze-fu Ching-po informed Confucius of it, saying, "Our master is certainly being led astray by the Kung-po Liao, but I have still power enough left to cut Liao off, and expose his corpse in the market and in the court."

items.name: The Master: If my principles are to advance, it is so ordered

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "If my principles are to advance, it is so ordered. If they are to fall to the ground, it is so ordered. What can the Kung-po Liao do where such ordering is concerned?"

items.name: The Master: Some men of worth retire from the world

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Some men of worth retire from the world. Some retire from particular states. Some retire because of disrespectful looks. Some retire because of contradictory language."

items.name: The Master: Those who have done this are seven men

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Those who have done this are seven men."

items.name: Tsze-lu: happening to pass the night in Shih-man, the gatekeeper s

sections.Teaching: Tsze-lu happening to pass the night in Shih-man, the gatekeeper said to him, "Whom do you come from?" Tsze-lu said, "From Mr. K'ung." "It is he,-is it not?"-said the other, "who knows the impracticable nature of the times and yet will be doing in them."

items.name: The Master: was playing, one day, on a musical stone in Weil when a

sections.Teaching: The Master was playing, one day, on a musical stone in Weil when a man carrying a straw basket passed door of the house where Confucius was, and said, "His heart is full who so beats the musical stone."

items.name: A little while after, he added, "How contemptible is the

sections.Teaching: A little while after, he added, "How contemptible is the one-ideaed obstinacy those sounds display! When one is taken no notice of, he has simply at once to give over his wish for public employment. 'Deep water must be crossed with the clothes on; shallow water may be crossed with the clothes held up.'"

items.name: The Master: How determined is he in his purpose

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "How determined is he in his purpose! But this is not difficult!"

items.name: Tsze-chang: What is meant when the Shu says that Kao-tsung, while ob

sections.Teaching: Tsze-chang said, "What is meant when the Shu says that Kao-tsung, while observing the usual imperial mourning, was for three years without speaking?"

items.name: The Master: Why must Kao-tsung be referred to as an example of this

sections.Teaching: The Master said, "Why must Kao-tsung be referred to as an example of this? The ancients all did so. When the sovereign died, the officers all attended to their several duties, taking instructions from the prime minister for three years."
